subject: The Hardest Game in the World: Founding a Startup
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://speedrun.substack.com/p/the-hardest-game-in-the-world-founding

💡  This Week’s Big Ideas
🧊  Applications for SR006 are open now through September 28th! Secure up to $1M for your startup by applying today [ https://substack.com/redirect/620eee92-fc2c-4444-97c4-1a73b3d9b0d8?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
🇨🇦  The a16z speedrun team is coming to Waterloo, Ontario. Register to join us [ https://substack.com/redirect/48d58dc4-1ad6-4a95-8a8a-b669c40c2798?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for an in-person dinner on Friday, September 26th. 
📦  Sam Shank explains why [ https://substack.com/redirect/e04baf83-e7ef-4db9-8310-d9add2612908?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] founders should “start by thinking about distribution.”
🏈  This Friday at 1:00pm PT Robin Guo and Ryan Rigney are hosting a live Q&A Office Hours with FanDuel co-founder Nigel Eccles. Repost Robin’s tweet [ https://substack.com/redirect/723f57eb-2c60-4a60-b46c-d4966b81a2c0?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] and sign up using the link to join the call when it goes live.
💼  Join our talent network [ https://substack.com/redirect/53ee994d-7608-4e9d-8019-453d992812d3?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more opportunities. If we see a fit for you, we'll intro you to relevant founders in the portfolio.
With under two weeks left to apply for speedrun’s class of SR006, we’re rolling out new IRL ads across San Francisco.
Echoing a famous ad campaign [ https://substack.com/redirect/48bc9845-dad0-480c-8634-ad234d81a27f?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] from decades past, the posters feature a call to “be like” four a16z speedrun alumni: Fundamental Research Labs’s Nico Christie [ https://substack.com/redirect/2bfbce94-4018-4279-ae44-b3b072bbc70a?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], SweatPals’s Mandi Zhou [ https://substack.com/redirect/816a1e3d-6d95-4343-8355-dddcb7af3f3c?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], Hedra’s Michael Lingelbach [ https://substack.com/redirect/cacfe481-5051-4ded-a2b4-d03a4ece9674?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], and Sitch’s Nandini Mullaji [ https://substack.com/redirect/aec6813c-7caa-4cdb-821f-f2bc53434f9a?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], each of whom has gone on to raise millions after graduating from SR. 
We believe there’s room for far more people to become founders. But that belief comes with a caveat: startups are hard. Successfully building a company from 0 to exit requires a level of resilience normally associated only with elite soldiers or athletes—people who dedicate their lives to a mission.
Even the most successful founders who’ve come through speedrun say it’s not for the faint of heart. Recently we caught up with Nico, Mandi, Michael, and Nandini to ask about this more brutal side of the startup journey. We asked: 
What’s been the hardest part of their journey so far?
What are the most expensive lessons they’ve had to learn? 
What strategies have they come up with to stay sane when things get tough?
What follows may sound like a long list of reasons you shouldn’t start a startup. But we hope it comes off as a more useful strategy guide for the wary instead. 
The Hardest Part of the Founding Journey
Nico Christie is obsessed with being the best in the world at things, and he’s tasted it before. As a college student at the University of Florida, he went semi-pro and became nationally renowned for winning basketball dunk contests. Especially given his 5’ 9” height, commentators always called out his “insane” aerial leaps.
Nico compares the challenges he’s taken on in his dunking career and now in his role as a founder to “slaying dragons.” 
“Getting into speedrun is sort of conquering your first dragon,” he says. “And then you realize the next dragon is way bigger. This is every time you do anything. Every next round, every new product, every new hire.” 
Nico says that after completing speedrun, things have only gotten harder.  “You just get like a ton of battle scars,” he says, “But it makes you stronger. The goal is just to not die.”  
By this stage of the journey with Fundamental, which recently raised a $33 million Series A [ https://substack.com/redirect/6d7f812a-c44d-446b-a58a-7f4e7c395daa?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] round, Nico says he’s starting to feel like he can take on whatever dragon comes next, even if it does come at a cost to his health: “I’m probably going to die early,” he deadpans. “But that’s the game.”
Michael Lingelbach of Hedra [ https://substack.com/redirect/17c30f8d-dd48-4d9b-8257-b81284842a2b?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] says that one of the hardest things about being a founder is the constant rejection you face along the journey: 
“You face rejection when recruiting, you face rejection when fundraising. Even if a team seems successful, they've all gone through a lot of rejection. And when you’re successful, there's a payoff—People that maybe rejected you before are suddenly calling you up, being like, ‘hey, I know I passed on that job a year ago when you were a seed-stage company, and now you're a Series A company. Let's talk. And that can be satisfying. 
“But more often you'll spend six to 10 hours  pouring your heart and soul out to someone selling them on your vision. And then like suddenly you realize they care more about the cash. That can be really demoralizing and can kind of cause you to lose a little bit of faith in Silicon Valley.”
—Michael Lingelbach, cofounder and CEO of Hedra
Nandini Mullaji of Sitch [ https://substack.com/redirect/b4dab763-bc5c-4543-bcd1-bed3e4cb85b2?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] knows the pain of startups well. Her first attempt at founding a startup built around a dating app didn’t work. She says the hardest lesson to learn from that experience was that sometimes it’s just about timing.
“It’s been two years almost to the day since I shut down my first dating app. And when I think about the journey, I just think that sometimes you can be too early or you can be too late. And it's actually not about you.
“You can put in all of the effort and manage all of the inputs. But sometimes the market situation is just so different from what it needs to be in order for you to succeed. And it's really hard because the type of people who are founders are people who believe that they can change the world. But sometimes you’re just not the right founder, at the right time, with the right idea. And I think that's probably the hardest thing about being a founder: knowing that everything is not fundamentally under your control.”
—Nandini Mullaji, cofounder of Sitch
Expensive Lessons
We asked the four founders the same question: “What’s the most expensive lesson you’ve had to learn as a founder?”
All four came back independently with variations on the same answer: hiring mistakes.
Mandi Zhou, cofounder and CPO at Sweatpals, recounted a hiring mistake that then took too long to fix. “My most expensive lesson was rushing a hire and then hesitating to act when the fit was clearly wrong,” she says. “I gave too much grace, hoping things would improve, and it hurt team morale and slowed execution.” 
Ultimately, Mandi says she had to recognize she had a blindspot as a leader. “Real kindness is being clear early, set expectations, give honest feedback fast, and if it’s not a fit, make the call. Caring personally means you don’t let someone stay in a role where they can’t succeed in.”
“You really have to optimize for the right leaders in your company early on. I think it's really tempting to promote individual contributors into leadership positions because they're really good at doing the work, but you have to realize that managing people and growing teams is a separate skill from being a really strong IC. At the same time, when you're an early-stage startup, you don't want to hire a bunch of career managers who don't know how to build zero to one.”
—Michael Lingelbach
Nico Christie recounted a similar experience he had, which occurred when he brought on an early senior hire that slowed down the speed of his team dramatically.
“All of a sudden our velocity went from shipping everything weekly, to needing six months for user testing,” Nico says. “We knew better. As soon as we ended that relationship everything turned around.”
When told that hiring mistakes had been the consistent answer from other founders to the question about expensive lessons, Nico nodded. “Yeah, that doesn’t surprise me,” he said. “You have to learn your own lessons, though, which is the problem.”
Maybe suffering through bad hiring choices is a lesson that every founder has to learn on their own. 
Nandini Mullaji put it more bluntly. When it comes to making important hires or choosing a co-founder, she says: “You're going to have a few ex-boyfriends before you find your husband.”  
Strategies for Staying Sane
So, assuming that all the warnings above have failed to scare you off and you still want to become a founder, what are some actionable things you can do to make the experience more bearable? 
Michael Lingelbach says that one of the best things founders can do is to just work to create a company culture where people are empowered to solve their own problems: 
“It’s really useful to use the Socratic method. I think one of the biggest failures of culture is if you just tell everyone the answer to everything. A lot of the early stage is about culture management. How do you keep people enthusiastic and bought in, especially when the product doesn't even exist yet? Part of that is that you have to be inspiring and paint the vision. But you also have to manage the process right. A lot of that is helping people come to the answer and feeling a strong sense of ownership over what they're doing. So I think this is something I still work on, but how can you empower people to come to the right answer versus just telling people what the right answer is?”
—Michael Lingelbach
Nandini Mullaji says there are three habits that have saved her sanity—and her relationship with her husband—as they both embark on founder journeys:
“I advise people to do three things:
I actually take a break from my phone. So I don't use social media from 8pm to 8am, and I’ve found that allows me to focus on what I'm actually doing.
If you have a partner, take a scheduled date night especially if your partner is also a founder. Because otherwise you will never see each other and you will never prioritize your relationship. My partner is also a founder. So we kind of joke that our whole company has Wednesday date night because I have Wednesday date night. Everyone knows I'm offline from 7 to 10pm.
The last thing: Get used to sending voice notes to your team. It is so much quicker and more efficient than writing long texts and emails and slacks. And I think that's just a great time-saving hack.”
—Nandini Mullaji
Mandi Zhou says to stay focused on the next step, and try to get in a workout:
“I get those emotional roller coasters sometimes, in the morning I feel I’m on the top of the world and then question myself and everything by afternoon and the imposter syndrome will creep in. I usually remind myself that this is not about me, my ego, it’s about the company and I should look forward and keep going because we have much bigger fish to fry. I try to stay focused on the next step forward instead of replaying doubts. Something that always works for me is to do a workout, even 30 minutes of workout or jogging outside can get me out of that head space, unstuck myself and see the bigger picture.”
—Mandi Zhou
Asked for any last advice for founders thinking about embarking on the startup journey, Nico Christie pauses. “There are going to be a lot of reasons for you to want to quit,” he says. “Unless you have a really good reason, you're going to fail.” 
“So can you say why you're doing it?”
That’s it for this week. Thanks again to Nico, Mandi, Michael, and Nandini for sharing their hard-earned wisdom with us. 
For more weekly dives into the world of early stage startups, subscribe to our newsletter below.
Get the a16z speedrun newsletter weekly 👇

subject: You just made a payment

[Legal Update] Google Vids being launched as Google Workspace core
 service starting April 29, 2025
body_text:
Google Workspace logo



Your users will have full access to generative AI in Google Vids for 12  
months.



Dear administrator,

We're writing to inform you that Google Vids, our AI-powered video creation  
application, will soon be included as a core service in your qualifying  
Google Workspace subscriptions.

We've provided additional information below to guide you through this  
change.

What this means for your organization

As early as April 29, 2025, Google Vids will start rolling out to users in  
your organization with the following Google Workspace subscriptions:


Google Workspace Business Starter
Google Workspace Enterprise Starter
Google Workspace for Nonprofits

This is in addition to Google Workspace editions, where Google Vids is  
already available as a core Workspace service.

Key changes:


Here are the some of the key changes resulting from this change for users  
accessing Google Vids with the above mentioned Google Workspace  
subscriptions:


Google Vids will be covered under your existing Google Workspace agreement  
and will receive the same technical support as other Google Workspace  
Services.
Your users will have full access to generative AI features in Google Vids  
for 12 months. These features include “Help me create,” AI voiceovers,  
read-along teleprompter, along with features such as “Generate a video”  
that were recently announced at Google Cloud Next 2025.
Access to these Generative AI features may be limited after the initial  
period. We'll notify you of any changes before they take effect.

What you need to do

No action is required from you to access Google Vids after this change is  
implemented.

We're here to help

For more information about this change, please refer to the following Help  
Center resources:


Learn about Google Vids availability
Turn Google Vids on or off for users

We know that data security, confidentiality, and compliance are top  
priorities for business leaders when adopting AI, and we are committed to  
helping you keep your data safe. Learn more about how your existing Google  
Workspace data protections are automatically applied to AI features.

Thanks for choosing Google Workspace.

– The Google Workspace Team




Was this information helpful?

A smiling face A neutral face A sad face


© 2025 Google LLC 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043

You have received this important update about your Google Workspace account  
because you designated this email address as a primary or secondary contact  
for mandatory service communications in your Google Partner Sales Console  
profile.

 Yay! This message means that your email account has been successfully connected to Woodpecker.co. The next step is adding prospects to your campaign. You can add prospects: Manually (when you have very few prospects to add); By importing a file; Using one of our native integrations with prospecting tools or via Zapier.  When it's all set up, just start your campaign and get new clients. Fingers crossed for your success! 

 The message landed in the Spam folder? 
 There is no need to worry, as this can happen. In such a case, simply move it to the Main Inbox and try to reconnect your account. 

 If you need any assistance or have any other questions, feel free to drop us a line at support@woodpecker.co.

Meghdad Farahmand
StemNLP GmbH
Meghdad Farahmand
Ebereschenallee 49
14050 Berlin

Details zur Mitgliedschaft:
Tarif: CoWoS Virtual Office - Business Pro | Kolonnenstr. 8 | 10827 Berlin, 35,00 EUR (+ 19.0% MwSt.)
Bedingungen und Konditionen:

Allgemeine Geschäftsbedingungen (Stand: Januar 2025)

Diese Allgemeinen Geschäftsbedingungen (AGB) gelten für sämtliche Leistungen der Valuedfriends Workspaces GmbH, Kolonnenstraße 8, 10827 Berlin („CoWoS“). Entgegenstehende oder ergänzende Geschäftsbedingungen des Vertragspartners („Kunde“) finden nur Anwendung, wenn CoWoS deren Geltung ausdrücklich und schriftlich zustimmt.

I.	Geltungsbereich und Leistungsumfang

CoWoS erbringt die vereinbarten Leistungen innerhalb der Geschäftszeiten (Montag bis Freitag, 9:00 bis 17:00 Uhr, ausgenommen gesetzliche Feiertage am Standort). Unterbrechungen infolge höherer Gewalt oder technischer Störungen sind hiervon ausgenommen.

II.	Vertragsgegenstand

1. CoWoS stellt entgeltlich Büroarbeitsplätze (fest zugewiesen oder flexibel), Postempfangsservices sowie Internetzugang über (W)LAN zur Verfügung. Je nach Bedarf können zusätzlich Meetingräume gebucht werden. Art und Umfang der Leistungen ergeben sich aus dem gewählten Tarif; dort sind auch die Preise ersichtlich.

2. An den Standorten befindet sich jeweils ein Bistro, das Getränke gegen Entgelt anbietet. Die jeweils gültigen Preise sind vor Ort ausgewiesen.

3. Der Vertrag wird persönlich mit dem Kunden geschlossen und ist nicht auf Dritte übertragbar.

III.	Rechte und Pflichten des Kunden

1. Je nach Tarif darf der Kunde die Anschrift des gewählten CoWoS-Standorts als Geschäftsadresse bzw. Firmensitz führen.

2. Kunden dürfen sich während der Geschäftszeiten in den CoWoS-Räumlichkeiten aufhalten und das Bistro nutzen. Arbeitsplätze stehen nur Kunden mit entsprechendem Tarif zur Verfügung. Bei Buchung eines Fix Desk oder Team Office erhält der Kunde einen Schlüssel und darf die Räume auch außerhalb der Geschäftszeiten nutzen.

3. Eingehende Post wird in einer verschließbaren Box hinterlegt.

4. Kunden mit Flex Desk-Tarif verpflichten sich, den Arbeitsplatz am Ende jedes Nutzungstags vollständig zu räumen.

5. Die Hausordnung ist bindend und hängt vor Ort aus. In sämtlichen Innenräumen – auch auf Balkonen und Innenhöfen – ist das Rauchen (inkl. E-Zigaretten) untersagt.

6. Die überlassenen Einrichtungsgegenstände und sonstige Gegenstände sind pfleglich zu behandeln; etwaige Schäden sind unverzüglich zu melden.

7. Die Nutzung der Geschäftsräume und Infrastruktur ist für rechtswidrige, sittenwidrige oder betrügerische Zwecke untersagt.

8. Der Kunde hat sicherzustellen, dass seine Geräte hinreichend gegen Viren und andere Sicherheitsrisiken geschützt sind, um Schäden an der Infrastruktur oder anderen Nutzern zu vermeiden.

9. CoWoS verpflichtet sich zur Vertraulichkeit. Informationen werden nur auf ausdrückliche Weisung des Kunden weitergegeben.

10. CoWoS ist nicht verpflichtet, Einschreiben oder Pakete mit Zusatzkosten entgegenzunehmen.

11. Für Inhalte von durch CoWoS im Auftrag versandten Nachrichten (Briefe, Faxe etc.) haftet ausschließlich der Kunde. Dieser stellt CoWoS von etwaigen Ansprüchen Dritter frei.

12. Nach Vertragsende sind sämtliche überlassenen Räume, Gegenstände, Schlüssel und Zugangskarten vollständig und im ordnungsgemäßen Zustand zurückzugeben.

IV.	Vertragsabschluss, Laufzeit und Kündigung

1. Verträge können per E-Mail, über das Online-Buchungstool von CoWoS oder vor Ort geschlossen werden.

2. Verträge können unbefristet oder mit einjähriger Laufzeit abgeschlossen werden.

3. Unbefristete Verträge sind jederzeit mit Monatsfrist kündbar. Jahresverträge können mit einer Frist von zwei Monaten zum Laufzeitende gekündigt werden. Erfolgt keine fristgerechte Kündigung, verlängert sich der Vertrag automatisch um ein weiteres Jahr.

4. CoWoS kann das Vertragsverhältnis fristlos kündigen, wenn ein wichtiger Grund vorliegt – insbesondere bei:

o Zahlungsverzug von über vier Wochen

o Nichtzahlung der Sicherheitsleistung

o schwerwiegenden Verstößen gegen die Hausordnung

o vertragswidrigem Verhalten oder Überlassung an Dritte

o strafrechtlich relevanter Geschäftstätigkeit

o grober Verletzung von Nebenpflichten

o Insolvenzeröffnung oder deren Ablehnung mangels Masse

5. Kündigungen können auch in Textform (z. B. per E-Mail) erfolgen. Eine Unterschrift in Papierform ist nicht erforderlich.

6. Bei fristloser Kündigung kann CoWoS dem Kunden den Zutritt zu den Räumlichkeiten untersagen, sofern dies zur Räumungsabwicklung nicht erforderlich ist.

V. Preisanpassung

1. CoWoS ist berechtigt, Preise anzupassen, wenn sich wesentliche Kostenfaktoren wie Energiepreise, Löhne oder behördliche Abgaben verändern.

2. Preisanpassungen müssen verhältnismäßig sein und dürfen den Kunden nicht unangemessen benachteiligen.

3. Änderungen werden mindestens vier Wochen vor Inkrafttreten in Textform mitgeteilt. Erfolgt kein Widerspruch innerhalb von zwei Wochen, gilt die Anpassung als genehmigt. Der Kunde wird in der Mitteilung auf das Widerspruchsrecht sowie die Folgen des Schweigens ausdrücklich hingewiesen.

VI. Zahlungsmodalitäten, Gebühren und Sicherheitsleistung

1. Zahlungen erfolgen per Kreditkarte oder SEPA-Lastschrift.

2. Bei Rücklastschriften werden neben Bankgebühren zusätzlich 10 € Bearbeitungsgebühr berechnet.

3. Bei Verlust oder nicht rechtzeitiger Rückgabe von Schlüsseln trägt der Kunde die Kosten für den Austausch der betroffenen Schlösser und ggf. Schließanlagen.

4. Die monatliche Servicemiete ist im Voraus zum Ersten eines jeden Monats fällig. Nichtnutzung der Leistung entbindet nicht von der Zahlungspflicht.

5. Zusatzleistungen werden separat abgerechnet und zusammen mit der Miete des Folgemonats fällig. Einwendungen sind binnen 7 Tagen nach Rechnungserhalt zu erheben.

6. Bei Zahlungsverzug verfallen gewährte Rabatte. Es gelten dann die regulären Tarife.

VII.	Haftung

1. Der Kunde haftet für alle Schäden, die durch ihn, seine Mitarbeiter oder Lieferanten verursacht werden – insbesondere bei Schäden durch unsichere technische Geräte, die das CoWoS-Netzwerk beeinträchtigen. In solchen Fällen stellt der Kunde CoWoS von etwaigen Forderungen Dritter frei.

2. CoWoS haftet bei Vorsatz oder grober Fahrlässigkeit – uneingeschränkt bei Schäden an Leben, Körper oder Gesundheit.

3. Ansprüche gegen CoWoS verjähren, wenn sie nicht innerhalb von drei Monaten nach Ablehnung durch CoWoS oder dessen Versicherung gerichtlich geltend gemacht werden.

VIII.	Änderungen beim Kunden

1. Änderungen der Rechtsform, Handelsregistereinträge oder Vertretungsverhältnisse sind CoWoS unverzüglich mitzuteilen.

2. Bei Wechsel in eine haftungsbeschränkte Rechtsform bleibt die persönliche Haftung des ursprünglichen Vertragspartners bestehen, sofern keine gesonderte Vereinbarung getroffen wird.

IX.	Vorsteuerabzug

Der Kunde sichert zu, zum vollen Vorsteuerabzug berechtigt zu sein. Sollte sich dies ändern, verpflichtet er sich, CoWoS unverzüglich zu informieren und etwaige daraus entstehende steuerliche Nachteile auszugleichen. In diesem Fall gilt die Miete als Bruttobetrag ohne separaten Umsatzsteuerausweis.

X.	Schriftform, AGB-Änderung und salvatorische Klausel

1. Änderungen dieses Vertrags bedürfen der Schriftform. Nebenabreden oder Ergänzungen sind nur wirksam, wenn CoWoS sie schriftlich bestätigt.

2. CoWoS behält sich vor, die AGB jederzeit zu ändern. Änderungen werden dem Kunden spätestens zwei Wochen vor Inkrafttreten in Textform mitgeteilt. Widerspricht der Kunde nicht fristgerecht, gelten die Änderungen als angenommen. Bei Widerspruch besteht der Vertrag unter den alten AGB fort; CoWoS kann jedoch mit Monatsfrist kündigen.

3. Sollte eine Bestimmung unwirksam sein oder werden, bleiben die übrigen Regelungen unberührt. Die Parteien verpflichten sich, eine der unwirksamen Regelung wirtschaftlich gleichwertige Ersatzregelung zu vereinbaren.

XI.	Gerichtsstand und anwendbares Recht

1. Gerichtsstand ist Berlin, sofern der Kunde Kaufmann ist.

2. Es gilt das Recht der Bundesrepublik Deutschland.

Wir werden dich benachrichtigen, sobald deine Mitgliedschaft bestätigt wurde. In der Zwischenzeit kannst du dich gerne an uns wenden, wenn du Fragen hast.

Du erhältst diese E-Mail, weil du dich für eine Mitgliedschaft bei CoWoS Berlin angemeldet hast.

CoWoS - eine Marke der Valuedfriends Workspaces GmbH

Kolonnenstr. 8
10827 Berlin

mail@cowos-berlin.com ( mailto:mail@cowos-berlin.com )

Datenschutzrichtlinie ( https://cowos-berlin.cobot.me/privacy-policy )

Impressum ( https://cowos-berlin.cobot.me/imprint )

Einloggen ( https://cowos-berlin.cobot.me/login )

# The Gemini vs ChatGPT Numbers Game: What the Data Actually Tells Us

Here's the thing about competitive narratives in tech: they're usually built on cherry-picked metrics that tell half the story. The recent claims about Google's Gemini "rapidly catching up" to ChatGPT? Classic example.

Let's break down what's actually happening.

## The Headlines Are Technically True

Yes, Gemini hit #1 on Apple's App Store in September 2025 after launching its viral "Nano Banana" image editing feature—12.6 million downloads that month, a 45% surge. Yes, users now spend an average of 7.2 minutes per session with Gemini versus 6 minutes with ChatGPT. And yes, Gemini overtook ChatGPT in Android installs back in late April 2025.

All verified. All accurate.

But here's the kicker: these metrics are hiding the actual competitive picture.

## What the Numbers Don't Tell You

While Gemini users spend longer per session, ChatGPT users come back 2.2x more often—12.74 visits per user versus Gemini's 5.73. That frequency gap matters enormously for building habit-forming products and long-term monetization.

The scale differential is even more revealing. ChatGPT maintains 190.6 million daily active users compared to Gemini's 35 million. That's a 5.4x advantage in people actually using these tools every single day. On the web, where users have genuine choice, ChatGPT commands 74% of chatbot traffic versus Gemini's 12.9%.

## The Distribution Advantage vs Product Preference Question

Google's real weapon isn't superior AI—it's distribution at scale. Gemini comes pre-installed on 2+ billion Android devices. It's baked into Google Search, Gmail, Workspace, and YouTube. When you're the default assistant on your own operating system, download numbers tell you more about convenience than preference.

The data backs this up: when Gemini became Android's default assistant in August 2025, usage of the OS overlay increased while standalone app downloads dropped 20%. Users were defaulting, not choosing.

## What Actually Shifted in 2025

Google figured out how to leverage its ecosystem to distribute AI at unprecedented scale. Gemini went from 90 million to 650 million monthly active users in twelve months—that growth is real and impressive. The viral Nano Banana feature proved Google can still create product moments that capture attention.

But when you look at intentional usage patterns—people deliberately opening an AI assistant to solve problems—ChatGPT remains dominant. Higher retention (89% among paying subscribers), more frequent visits, and far more weekly minutes spent on the platform.

## The Takeaway

The competition has genuinely intensified. Sam Altman's reported "code red" directive in December 2025 reflects legitimate pressure from Gemini 3's benchmark performance. Google is winning specific battles in downloads and viral moments.

But framing this as parity or reversal? That misses the story. The real question isn't whether Gemini is catching up—it's whether forced distribution can eventually create genuine product preference. The data suggests we're not there yet.


———

https://www.aiweekly.com/p/is-google-about-to-surpass-opena-i

subject: Following up – Calibrion AI Seed Round
body_text:
Dear team at Lunar Ventures,

I hope this message finds you well.

I wanted to follow up on my previous email regarding Calibrion AI and our EUR 3 million seed round. We continue to see strong momentum, with pilot-stage interest from multiple companies in Europe and the US, confirming the clear need for enterprise-grade LLM customization based on proprietary data.

Given Lunar Ventures’ focus on deep tech and early-stage technical breakthroughs, I believe Calibrion aligns strongly with your investment thesis. I would love to share our pitch deck and arrange a short call to walk you through our live product, our traction so far, and how we plan to scale.

Would you have time next week for a quick conversation? I’m happy to adjust to your availability.

Looking forward to your thoughts and hoping we can connect soon.

Best regards,
Nils

--
Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 ・ nils@calibrion.ai <>
Französische Str. 20・10117 Berlin・Germany
www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>

￼￼

> On 22. Jul 2025, at 10:22, Nils Erbach <nils@calibrion.ai> wrote:
> 
> Dear team at Lunar Ventures,
> 
> I am reaching out to introduce Calibrion AI, a data annotation platform enabling enterprises and organisations to fine-tune LLMs to their proprietary data. We built a working product and already reached pilot-stage interest from European and US companies in the eCom vertical.
> 
> While most LLM infra and dev platforms focus on model architecture and LLMOps, we focus on unlocking enterprise adoption through (proprietary) data and fine-tuning workflows.
> 
> Calibrion envisions a future where businesses harness their proprietary data and domain expertise to build specialized LLMs, turning generative AI from a threat into a strategic asset for both internal and market-facing applications.
> 
> Our mission is to become a market-leading platform for LLM customization and domain adaptation, setting the standard for performance and usability in the enterprise AI space.
> 
> To underscore the scale of this opportunity and the importance of what Calibrion does: In June 2025, Meta acquired a 49% stake in Scale AI (a company also focused on LLM data annotation and founded in 2016) for $14.8 billion at a $30 billion valuation. 
> 
> We are currently offering subscription for our EUR 3 m seed investment round to: 
> - expand our engineering and data science team
> - Accelerate go-to-market efforts across verticals 
> - Further develop our AI capabilities and integrate new LLM-driven features.
> 
> To accelerate the company's development, we assigned significant equity to an option pool for key employees and aim to be cash-flow positive within 18 months.
> 
> We would be happy to share our pitch deck and schedule a call to walk you through the live product. The product is fully developed and currently undergoing regular testing and stabilization.
> 
> Please find attached a letter outlining our fund-raising goals as well as a one-pager on Calibrion.
> 
> We look forward to hearing from you and would be glad to schedule a conversation at your convenience.
> 
> Best regards,
> Nils
> 
> -- 
> Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
> Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 <tel:+49+(0)30+478+662+25> ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>
> Französische Str. 20・10117 Berlin・Germany
> www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>
> 
>

subject: Re: Thank You for the Cotopaxi Connection - Let's Catch Up Soon
body_text:
Meghdad,

Very sorry for the delay - and of course, I hope that it works out to
create a good project for you and the Cotopaxi team.  It's clear they could
benefit from some of the AI tools you can build!

As for thoughts for how the project comes together, just keep me in the
loop on how the conversations with the team are going and I can help nudge
things along to keep moving forward.  The Cotopaxi team has a lot on its
plate, so just let me know if things start to feel delayed.  There is a new
head of IT joining in a few weeks too (that will help).

Other than that, things are good here.  Very busy, but good.  Hope we're
able to find another project to work on together with Cotopaxi, and I'll
keep my eye out for other opportunities that come together too.  I believe
there are lots of future use cases across the portfolio, just need to get
clearer on priorities and where we're ready to invest capital to bring the
projects to life.

Jacob

wrote:

subject: Thank You for the Cotopaxi Connection - Let's Catch Up Soon
body_text:
Hi Jacob,

I wanted to thank you for introducing us to Cotopaxi. Such cool products
and such an impactful company! There’s clearly room for integrating LLMs to
help optimize how their product images are presented online similar to the
work we’re doing with Zalando.

How are things going on your end?
Let's catch up soon!

Also, if you have any thoughts or wishes for how this project should shape
up with Coropoxi, I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Warm regards,
Meghdad

body_text:
Dear all,

I hope you had a good weekend!

I am following up on our meeting from Thursday, August 21st.

We are eager to learn if you have reached a decision regarding the
discussed pilot project with Calibrion to test how Calibrion can help with
the quality of AI-based image and color generation for your product images.

Please let us know if you require any further information from our end.

Best regards,
Meghdad

subject: Cotopaxi x Calibrion – Meeting Follow-up & Next Steps
body_text:
Dear all,

Thank you again for your time and for the great discussion. As a quick
wrap-up, here are the key points from our meeting:

- You will clarify internally how to establish a guideline/standard for
measuring image quality.

- We can provide custom features in the enterprise tier, such as prompting
to re-generate images based on feedback, color pallette etc. as well as
other requested features.

- If you are ready to start with Calibrion, we look forward to beginning
with a pilot to test image and color generation focusing on the
quality of a subset of backpack parts.

Would it be possible for you to reach a decision by late next week?

Best regards,
Meghdad
-- 

Meghdad Farahmand PhD

Founder ・ Calibrion AI

Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 <+49+(0)30+478+662+25> ・ meghdad@calibrion.ai

Kolonnenstr. 8・10827 Berlin・Germany

www.calibrion.ai

subject: Red Sift Quarterly Vol. 4 🌷
body_text:
͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ 
͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ 
͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ ͏‌ 

Red Sift Quarterly vol 4 ( https://redsift.com/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

---------------------------------------
Springing into Q2 with powerful updates
---------------------------------------

Spring is in the air—and so are new innovations, feature upgrades, and major milestones from the Red Sift team. Whether you’re following Microsoft’s bulk sender requirements, wondering about Zoom’s certificate renewal outage, or keen to find out why Red Sift OnDMARC is number #1 in EMEA and Europe, we’ve got you covered.

Keep reading for what’s fresh this quarter

Mail icon  Navigating Microsoft’s bulk email changes
-------------------------------------------------------

If you're sending over 5,000 emails a day to Microsoft inboxes, new bulk sender guidelines are in effect. Don’t risk getting your emails rejected or spam-foldered. Check if you’re compliant now with our free Red Sift Investigate tool ( https://redsift.com/tools/investigate?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ).

Bulk up or Bust: Meeting Microsoft's New Requirements for high volume senders Thursday ( https://app.livestorm.co/redsift/bulk-up-or-bust-meeting-microsofts-new-requirements-for-high-volume-senders?s=d8915d89-f916-464f-b14f-946a2d04e207&utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )
You can also join us May 8th for a live session breaking down Microsoft’s policy changes—and how to keep your outbound email secure and compliant. ( https://app.livestorm.co/redsift/bulk-up-or-bust-meeting-microsofts-new-requirements-for-high-volume-senders?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

Attend live ( https://app.livestorm.co/redsift/bulk-up-or-bust-meeting-microsofts-new-requirements-for-high-volume-senders?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

Award icon  Red Sift OnDMARC is #1 in EMEA and Europe.
-------------------------------------------------------

We’re proud to announce that Red Sift OnDMARC is the #1 Regional Leader in EMEA and Europe, while leading in 18 G2 Spring 2025 reports around the globe, earning 14 badges. 

It's thanks to your support—and glowing reviews—that we continue to top the charts. Check out all the details ( https://blog.redsift.com/news/red-sift-ondmarc-ranked-number-one-in-emea-and-europe-by-g2/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ) and see what our users are saying.

eye icon  Logo detection enhancements for Red Sift Brand Trust
---------------------------------------------------------------

The Brand Trust team has rolled out a fresh set of upgrades to our logo detection engine, improving accuracy and performance across the board. Red Sift’s hybrid AI approach enhances detection precision, effectively balancing the reduction of false positives with the identification of genuine threats. Better monitoring means better protection against impersonation—and more trust in your brand. Learn more about our hybrid approach ( https://blog.redsift.com/ai/enhanced-logo-detection-with-ai-a-hybrid-approach/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ).

cloud and lighting icon  When Zoom stopped zooming
---------------------------------------------------

Earlier this month, Zoom’s certificate renewal outage sent ripples across the internet—blocking user access and leaving IT teams scrambling. The takeaway? Passive monitoring isn't enough.

Whether it's a household name or your own domain, certificate oversight can cost real downtime. We broke down what happened, how it could’ve been avoided, and why proactive certificate monitoring is now a must-have. Read the full breakdown ( https://blog.redsift.com/asm/zoom-stops-zooming-why-active-monitoring-is-essential/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ).

brain icon  latest thought leadership
--------------------------------------

The Story Behind OnDMARC
------------------------

CEO Rahul Powar shares his thoughts on the innovation behind Red Sift OnDMARC—and what’s driving its continued momentum in the fight against phishing and Business Email Compromise (BEC). Read the full reflection ( https://blog.redsift.com/email/dmarc/reflecting-on-the-development-of-red-sift-ondmarc/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

Preparing for a Post-Quantum World
----------------------------------

Quantum computing is no longer science fiction—and it’s already pushing the boundaries of traditional encryption. Our experts break down what Post-Quantum Cryptography (PQC) means for the internet and WebPKI, why it matters now, and what security leaders should do to prepare. Get quantum-ready ( https://blog.redsift.com/cybersecurity/post-quantum-cryptography-for-internet-and-webpki/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

calender icon  Connect with Red Sift
-------------------------------------

screen icon Microsoft Bulk Mail Changes ( https://app.livestorm.co/redsift/bulk-up-or-bust-meeting-microsofts-new-requirements-for-high-volume-senders?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )Webinar ( https://app.livestorm.co/redsift/bulk-up-or-bust-meeting-microsofts-new-requirements-for-high-volume-senders?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ) - May 8 | Virtual

pin icon Cisco Live 2025 ( https://www.ciscolive.com/global.html?zid=pp&utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ) - June 8 - 14 | San Diego, California
pin icon Pax8 Beyond 2025 ( https://www.pax8beyond.com/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ) - June 8 - 10 | Denver, Colorado
pin icon CyberTech Europe ( https://italy.cybertechconference.com/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing ) - October 21 - 22 | Rome, Italy

screen icon  Hot off the press
-------------------------------

* Red Sift joins the Jisc Chest platform to strengthen email security in the education sector ( https://blog.redsift.com/news/red-sift-ondmarc-joins-the-jisc-chest-platform/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )
* Mailgun and Red Sift partner to boost email programs with stronger authentication ( https://blog.redsift.com/email/mailgun-and-red-sift-partner-to-boost-email-programs-with-stronger-authentication/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )
* Red Sift named a Top 50 company in 2025 Emerging Stars Awards ( https://blog.redsift.com/awards/red-sift-named-a-top-50-company-in-2025-emerging-stars-awards/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )
* Red Sift wins 2025 Cybersecurity Excellence Award for OnDMARC ( https://blog.redsift.com/awards/red-sift-wins-2025-cybersecurity-excellence-award-for-ondmarc/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )
* Why DMARC and BIMI are a business priority ( https://blog.redsift.com/bimi/why-dmarc-and-bimi-are-a-business-priority/?utm_campaign=RedSiftQuarterlyVol4&utm_content=Red+Sift+Quarterly+Vol+3&utm_medium=email_action&utm_source=email_marketing )

We hope this edition of Red Sift Quarterly brings you fresh insights and even fresher reasons to keep your cybersecurity posture strong.

Until next time,
Team Red Sift

🔍  Troy Kirwin has a strategy for finding “a bunch of boring business services co’s or legacy software that AI will light a torch to.” [ https://substack.com/redirect/828227f6-cd7d-4cec-b57f-7f10b3902444?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]
😡  Andrew Lee shares “the 10 things investors secretly hate about your pitch.” [ https://substack.com/redirect/813ca827-9933-4f1f-9a64-1ecb3554875b?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]
❌  Jon Lai has advice for founders for when VCs pass on a pitch [ https://substack.com/redirect/f0a4eb90-6d83-4244-b9cc-2421fc3a166a?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
💼  Join our talent network [ https://substack.com/redirect/2e8be696-b4de-4814-8c3e-b17014ae42ca?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more opportunities. If we see a fit for you, we'll intro you to relevant founders in the portfolio.
Twice a year, a16z speedrun founders gather in San Francisco for the culminating event of our 12-week startup program: Demo Day.
The format is simple: founders take the stage before a curated audience of hundreds to pitch their startup in only two minutes. It can be a thrilling—and not a little nerve-wracking—affair for the founders, many of whom will use Demo Day as a chance to turbocharge the launch of their startups.
Last Tuesday morning, the big moment had arrived for the class of SR005. The founders and an audience of over 1,600 attendees gathered in SF’s Yerba Buena Center for the Arts. 
After checking in, the audience of tech luminaries settled into their seats in the Blue Shield of California Theater. 
>From here, a16z General Partner Andrew Chen welcomed the guests and kicked off the day’s main event: two straight hours of back-to-back live pitches.
One of the first founders to present was Elena Wagenmans [ https://substack.com/redirect/4c128157-2639-4332-b0fd-6bc1f5c85ae6?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], cofounder and CEO of Taya [ https://substack.com/redirect/8c2d3096-fc86-4793-b481-fe67b837abb2?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. Wagenmans and her team were feeling great about the viral launch of their AI wearable [ https://substack.com/redirect/3e566d68-ade7-4377-9786-b87b87567ec0?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], but says she was “definitely nervous at first.” 
Wagenmans, a former squash player and gold medalist at the European Team Championships, says the solution was to treat prep for her pitch the same way she prepared for high-pressure sports matches in the past.
“Once I reframed it like game day, the adrenaline turned into focus,” she says. “Afterward, I mostly felt relieved and proud that the story landed the way I hoped!” 
The fifth team to present was Anchr [ https://substack.com/redirect/3c09024d-eac2-44b9-881e-7553836cbc1c?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], led by cofounder Tzar Taraporvala [ https://substack.com/redirect/f562e635-54b7-48bd-bf1e-b2694be33a26?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. As he had during practice sessions, Taraporvala brought his trademark “11 out of 10” energy level to his pitch for an AI-powered operating system for food distributors. 
Zoe Lynch [ https://substack.com/redirect/918cbe92-7ca4-434d-9cab-f5ef3d665fb4?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], founder and CEO of  [ https://substack.com/redirect/a9ca3167-5fd5-4f18-a8eb-72626853517f?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]Variant AI [ https://substack.com/redirect/a9ca3167-5fd5-4f18-a8eb-72626853517f?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], won over the crowd with her pitch for Variant, an AI-powered tool to lets people express themselves by making games.
“I’ve always loved stories,” she says, “but growing up, I struggled to tell my own. With multiple processing differences, words often failed me. That’s why I turned to making games: They told the stories I couldn’t, with even more impact.”
Even after the pitches were done, the day wasn’t over. Founders filtered over to the YBCA forum for a chance to meet in person at demo stations where guests could experience their products for themselves.
“After the pitch, the adrenaline didn’t stop because we had demo stations right after!” says Madeline Park [ https://substack.com/redirect/6014b1a6-e797-4029-aa8d-5160db37f197?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], Cofounder & CEO of Vega [ https://substack.com/redirect/08d413b5-e1a3-4f2e-b08c-5289cd44366e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], a social network where you post about your friends instead of about yourself. 
Park, who grew up running track and cross country, said that the moments before Demo Day reminded her of the moments before a big race: “Excitement, anticipation, hyper-focus, and the quite knowing that at least in that moment, the hard work had already been done. But more than anything, I felt gratitude,” she says, “for the opportunity to share Vega with the world, for the folks who helped us get here, and gratitude for my cofounder and myself for the sheer grit we’ve put into our company.”
Maxwell Wang [ https://substack.com/redirect/0abfa60a-0945-4bbc-a61a-77ade11acabd?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], cofounder and CEO of Dispatcher [ https://substack.com/redirect/2415e2a2-d7ac-437c-a0b5-bf89a3a35f14?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], was also in a grateful mood. He said the thing about Demo Day that stuck out for him most personally was the “super collaborative” energy backstage and in the demo hall with the other founders.
“It came across more like a team event than a competition,” Wang says, “with everyone hyping each other up and keeping the energy high.” 
But of course, the speedrun journey doesn’t end with Demo Day. Many of the teams presenting last week follow it up immediately by continuing to build their companies and establishing relationships to help them grow.
The timing works well, as gathering together at an event like this in San Francisco creates “an incredible catalyst for connection and collaboration,” according to Alexis Sursock [ https://substack.com/redirect/3eee8a63-17a5-4d0a-9a83-20f42b41cbae?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], founder and CEO of Luvu [ https://substack.com/redirect/74fd8c93-abdf-4df4-bc67-1f1e5a4c66be?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. “Anyone we wanted to share ideas with was there, and instead of having to chase introductions, the conversations happened naturally.”
After Demo Day wrapped, there were more events to go to, like the “AI Faire” hosted at a16z’s San Francisco offices. There, over 30 companies from the SR005 cohort had yet another chance to demo their products for buyers from top tech companies.
SF Tech Week wrapped up with a flurry of dinners, meetups, panels, and hangouts. 
If you missed it this year, there’s always next time.

subject: Seed Round Subscription Opportunity Calibrion AI
body_text:
Dear team at Lunar Ventures,

I am reaching out to introduce Calibrion AI, a data annotation platform enabling enterprises and organisations to fine-tune LLMs to their proprietary data. We built a working product and already reached pilot-stage interest from European and US companies in the eCom vertical. 

We are currently offering subscription for our EUR 3 m seed investment round to: 
- expand our engineering and data science team
- Accelerate go-to-market efforts across verticals 
- Further develop our AI capabilities and integrate new LLM-driven features.

To accelerate the company's development, we assigned significant equity to an option pool for key employees and aim to be cash-flow positive within 18 months.

We would be happy to send over our pitch-deck and arrange a call where a walk-through of the actual product can be arranged. 

Please find attached a letter outlining our fund-raising goals as well as a one-pager on Calibrion.

We would be pleased to hear from you and would welcome any additional feedback.

Kind regards, 
Nils 

-- 
Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 <tel:+49+(0)30+478+662+25> ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>
Französische Str. 20・10117 Berlin・Germany
www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>

￼￼

subject: IMPORTANT: Price change for calibrion.ai
body_text:
From: "The Google Workspace Team" [workspace-noreply@google.com]
Subject:  IMPORTANT: Price change for calibrion.ai

Change to subscription price

## The pricing for your Google Workspace Business Starter subscription will  
change beginning July 9, 2025

Dear Google Workspace administrator,

We're writing to inform you of the price change that will go into effect  
for your Google Workspace Business Starter subscription on or after July 9,  
2025. The updated subscription pricing reflects the significant added AI  
value, as well as the many new features we have introduced and are  
launching to Google Workspace editions. Learn more  
(https://workspace.google.com/blog/product-announcements/empowering-businesses-with-AI?utm_source=15509171&utm_medium=email).

Please visit the Admin console Billing page  
(https://admin.google.com/ac/billing/subscriptions?utm_source=15509171&utm_medium=email)  
to review your new subscription price. Learn more about billing  
(https://support.google.com/a/topic/6142420?utm_source=15509171&utm_medium=email).

### We're here to help

If you have any questions, visit the Google Workspace Admin Help Center  
(https://support.google.com/a/topic/6142420?utm_source=15509171&utm_medium=email).  
You can also contact Google Workspace support  
(https://support.google.com/a/answer/1047213?utm_source=15509171&utm_medium=email)  
if you require assistance.

Thank you for choosing Google Workspace. We value and appreciate your  
business.

Sincerely,

The Google Workspace Team


(C) 2025  Google LLC 1600 Amphitheatre Parkway, Mountain View, CA 94043 *   
Google Asia Pacific Pte. Ltd., 8 Marina View, #30-01, Asia Square 1,  
Singapore 018960  *  Google Cloud EMEA Ltd., 70 Sir John Rogersons Quay,  
Dublin 2, Ireland.

You're receiving this mandatory email service announcement to update you  
about important changes to your Google Workspace product or account.

body_text:
Qonto ( https://qonto.com/en?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=header&utm_content=website&utm_position=block_header_logo )

What’s new with your Qonto account?

No new system to learn, no workflow to change - it’s business as usual, only faster.

Transactions view ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_1_img )

Hello Meghdad,

You may have noticed something different the last time you checked your Qonto account.

Your new, interactive transactions view now lets you do so much more. It’s pure productivity, where every detail is yours to control.

Go granular and get the full context on each transaction, or zoom out for the bigger picture of your business finances - all without leaving the page.

Transactions on your terms ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_2_img )

Transactions on your terms

No more rigid layouts or switching between screens. The dynamic transactions view lets you attach documents with a click, update VAT details & see your financial data exactly how you want it.

Manage your transactions ( https://app.qonto.com/detectapp.html?appUrl=deeplinks%3Faction%3Dtransactions.index%26organization_slug%3Dstemnlp-gmbh-4570%26cta%3Dcta2 )

Find out more ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_2_cta )

Customize your view ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_3_img )

A custom view that matches your thinking

Drag & drop columns & hide what doesn't matter. How much easier would business finance admin be if you could see everything you needed, laid out in exactly the way that works for your business?

Customize your view ( https://app.qonto.com/detectapp.html?appUrl=deeplinks%3Faction%3Dtransactions.index%26organization_slug%3Dstemnlp-gmbh-4570%26cta%3Dcta3 )

Find out more ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_3_cta )

Business as usual, only faster ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_4_img )

Business as usual, only faster

Get more done with the tools you already know. No new system or workflow to learn - it’s how you usually manage your business finances, just more efficient. Take one-click action, right from the transaction details.

Start working efficiently ( https://app.qonto.com/detectapp.html?appUrl=deeplinks%3Faction%3Dtransactions.index%26organization_slug%3Dstemnlp-gmbh-4570%26cta%3Dcta4 )

Find out more ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/31990454370321-How-does-the-Transaction-view-work?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=modular_tables&utm_content=FAQ_modular_tables&utm_position=block_4_cta )

Help center ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=signature&utm_content=FAQ_en&utm_position=block_signature_img )

Have a question?

Take a look at our help center or contact us by chat from your account.

Go to help center ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=signature&utm_content=FAQ_en&utm_position=block_signature_cta )

Qonto ( https://qonto.com/en )

We collect and process your personal data so we can send you updates on products and industry news. Learn more ( https://qonto.com/en/legal-documents/privacy-notice ) about how we manage your data and your rights.

subject: Andrew Chen's “Dinner Party Jerk” Test — Why Founders Need to Pitch Themselves Harder
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://speedrun.substack.com/p/andrew-chen-dinner-party-jerk-test

It’s the final week. Applications for the class of SR006 close on September 28, 2025 at 11:59 PM PT. Apply today [ https://substack.com/redirect/0125c583-1997-4ded-b160-d752dfcecc7d?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for a shot at securing up to $1M for your startup. 
💡  This Week’s Big Ideas
🧊  Join us in SF for Demo Day! We handpicked 60+ new startups with founders from OpenAI, DeepMind, Meta, Uber, Anduril, NASA, Palantir, Stanford, Oxford, MIT & more. Apply here [ https://substack.com/redirect/e5fa46de-1627-4571-a5ea-457b6735c7b4?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
🗣️  Got questions about speedrun? Come chat with us. Join our live Ask Us Anything Q&A call with the SR team this Thursday at 5:00pm PT. Register here [ https://substack.com/redirect/d539ffa5-ef4d-43ef-94e5-fbe7dc11f6e9?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. 
🗓️  The official calendar for SF/LA Tech Week is now live. Choose from over 1,500 free events including fundraising events, hackathons, demo days and more: Register at tech-week.com/calendar [ https://substack.com/redirect/d48a9765-e667-4a87-b696-1f6d93536a7f?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]
🤝  a16z speedrun gives you up to $1M in funding, but how does it help with traction? Head of BD Macy Mills explains [ https://substack.com/redirect/bf076bca-05eb-4aa1-8eaf-063e97b5d0f8?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
🃏  In a recent viral essay, Robin Guo argues that life is more like Poker than Chess [ https://substack.com/redirect/fe608339-a9cd-405c-af0d-51994eab9489?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
💼  Join our talent network [ https://substack.com/redirect/2b3bdce4-0b70-4df2-9f35-2b6eca2efec5?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more opportunities. If we see a fit for you, we'll intro you to relevant founders in the portfolio.
With just over two weeks to go until the class of SR005 presents at Demo Day in San Francisco, the heat is on for founders to hone and refine their presentations. 
This weekend, a16z speedrun General Partner Andrew Chen sent an email to the entire class calling out common issues he’s seeing in pitch decks. 
“Brag about your team, and add details,” Andrew wrote:
Many of you are long-winded and vague when you talk about yourselves. You can do better.
When you have your team slide, I suggest keeping it terse and to the point (we don't need to know you are childhood friends). Make sure you really brag about yourself. In particular, many of you are missing the ability to add specific facts and figures. If you say that you started a successful company previously, then tell me that you went from zero to X million in revenue. If you worked on a known product, tell me what you did there, and how many millions of users did you serve? Adding little bits of data helps make your resume even more impressive. I have a longer read on this topic here which discusses why you need to talk about your background in a way that is different than in "real life" — Be "the dinner party jerk.”
Andrew’s “Dinner Party Jerk” essay—originally published in his newsletter [ https://substack.com/redirect/b35d5807-efca-48b8-a136-8a18ec453f08?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] in early 2024—is worth reading for anyone who struggles with touting their own credentials. So today, we’re republishing it in full in the newsletter. Find it in full below. 
The “Dinner Party Jerk” test is a solution to a common problem:
Startups often struggle at pitching their team, even though for the earliest stage companies, it’s incredibly important to do it well to raise capital—as I’ve described it below:
Pre-seed - Bet on the team ‍
Seed - Bet on the product
Series A - Bet on the traction
Series B - Bet on the revenue
Series C - Bet on the unit economics
To figure out if you are properly pitching yourself in your team, run the thought experiment of describing yourselves at a dinner party. If you are pitching yourself hard, then if you are a kind human, you will turn red and blush with wild embarrassment. The reason is that a proper pitch includes many of your credentials, your achievements, the ways in which you and your team are highly unique, and we simply don’t talk like this at dinner parties. And yet this is exactly what you should do when you talk to investors, partners, customers, and potential employees.
A few years back, a big group of Nordic founders came by Silicon Valley. When I asked them their biggest learning on the trip so far, they said, “We have to learn how to pitch our startup in the ‘American’ way. More self promotional, emphasizing the future not the past, talking about what it could be not what it is, playing up even small bits of proof points, etc. Describing usage and telling stories, not just revenue.” 
They told me the investors back home didn’t care for this style.
Don’t hold back
Be the dinner party jerk, pitch yourself hard. Don’t hold back. Your shyness and cordiality is not helping.
I find that most founders tend to focus primarily on describing their idea to the exclusion of everything else. I’ve heard thousands of “elevator pitches” and they generally focus on the idea and not the team, the market, differentiation, or anything else. And they downplay their achievements or omit them.
Of course what you emphasize depends on your background. It’s often said that there are repeat founders and first time founders. But there’s another axis which is about obvious credentialing versus not obvious credentialing. For first time founders starting a gaming company, for instance, but who have already spent years at a top company in the field, a quick modification to the elevator pitch mentioning these credentials is both beneficial and quite obvious. But what do you do if you’re an uncredentialed first time founder?
Then the question becomes, what is your “earned secret“ behind the idea? Having a pithy story about how you were a Shopify seller, and that’s how you got to building any commerce product, is incredibly helpful. And if you have some metrics or an observation about the market that’s non-obvious, showing your expertise in the field is even more valuable. If you have various achievements either professional or academic or in an open source setting, it might be worth working those in even if they’re not directly related.
The other very awkward thing is to use facts and figures to describe yourself. If in your previous work, you worked on an app that served millions of people, or for your current company, you recently launched and got your first 10,000 users, you should include these numbers. Any traction and any validation is incredibly helpful proving your case. And of course, this is another thing that would make you a dinner party jerk.
Why don’t we do a better job of this? The dangers of conformity
You might be going through a moment of introspection now and asking, why am I like this? Why do I downplay achievements when I should be amping them up?
My answer to this is: it’s conformity. In real life, we often subconsciously conform to the people around us. If you go off at a friendly gathering about all the cool stuff you’ve done, and why you’re going to be great, there’s a fear that you’re exaggerating the differences between yourself and others. There’s a fun theory from evolutionary psychology that shyness is an evolved trait to keep us safe in a world where we grew up and tribes of a few hundred people, and a few wrong words might follow us around for our lifetimes.
This is also my theory for why people are reluctant to engage on social media and share their knowledge, when it’s obvious that it might be very helpful to them professionally.
TLDR; there’s pitch mode and dinner party mode. Learn to turn the former mode on!
Be an optimist
You have to be an optimist about your own product, your own startup, and yourself. That’s why when you pitch—whether to investors, to prospective employees, or partners, it’s important to talk about what might happen, not what you are doing today.
There’s a whole style to this type of pitch, and it’s a futuristic point of view that leans into optimism:
Emphasize the future, not the past
What it could be not what it is
Play up even small bits of proof points
The big things that might happen, if it works
The upside rather than risks
Signs the customers love the product, rather than revenue metrics
Why this team has the grit and special knowledge to do it, not the credentials and work experience
A unique narrative about why the world is moving this way
Why you’re starting with a wedge, but your ultimate market is huge
I previously referenced the idea that international founders often describe this as the “American” way of pitching—the funny thing about this is that this isn’t the “American” way of pitching. It’s actually specific to the Bay Area tech ecosystem. It’s incredibly optimistic and futuristic that founders choose to describe their startups in this way, and furthermore, the people who hear these pitches choose to believe them.
Why this is the only way to pitch investors, employees, and partners
Let me also make the argument that this is the only logical way to convince people to join you on your journey.
1. Investors
First, let’s talk about startup investors. A portfolio of startup investments is inherently risky, and the physics of venture math mean that the winners have to be really big. It’s commonly said that out of a portfolio of ten companies, generally about half the investments will go to zero, three will return a little bit of money, and that the top one or two will return 10x plus and make the fund work. As a result, professional venture investors are trying to understand if you have what it takes to be one of those top two, and if you don’t you’ll die trying. A lot of this assessment focuses on the market or your numbers, but sometimes the real question is about your ambition.
So investors are trying to answer a simple question: Do you WANT to create one of the leading companies in the industry?
Focusing on the future and on the upside shows your will to power. It allows investors to gain a sense of that signal. If you’re focused too early on profitability rather than growth, or on retaining your piece of the pie as opposed to growing the pie as large as possible, that’s an important signal. The point of this isn’t to mislead investors into thinking that you’re trying to do something that you’re not, but rather, if you are shooting for something big, you have to really express that in the clearest way possible.
This perspective is often directly reflected (or not) in the slide decks I review at a16z. Does the product slide describe the features of what the app has today, or does it talk about the product roadmap of what’s going to be built in the future? Do the user projections or financial forecasts simply show last year’s performance, or does it tell a story about how the business is about to inflect? Oftentimes when founders are too conservative about their story it’s hard for investors to understand what they’re trying to do in the long run. Instead, I love it when founders tell the big story. Of course I’m going to discount it and round down, and assume that many features are never shipped. But I love to see it.
2. Employees
Second, let’s talk about employees. Typically when you’re hiring your first few employees, you might be able to give out a few percentage points each, particularly for key people (like engineers or designers). But within a few hires, you end up needing to convince people to work for below pay, and for a fraction of a percent of the company. Why would they do this? Why would they work for you instead of either starting their own company or getting a cushy gig somewhere else where they might be paid much more?
The asymmetric advantage of startups compared to many other opportunities is that they are adventurous and fun. The startup might fail, but the work is generally a lot more interesting than what you can do elsewhere. The responsibility and scope that a junior employee might have may go way beyond what makes sense at any other company.
And of course financially there is upside. For founders to convince high-quality employees to join their outfit, it’s often important to lean into a sense of adventure. What’s more adventurous than tackling a big huge goal, that might not work, but if it works, it’s going to be amazing? For founders to communicate the sense of adventure, they need to be able to weave a narrative. Maybe it’s us versus them, or David and Goliath. Perhaps it’s exploring the unknown, and going to the frontier when no one else is there. If you can’t tell the stories, how can you expect people to follow you? Thus I find it important to tell the futuristic narrative that’s ambitious, full of surprises and upside, and has a possibility of failure too. It makes the work meaningful and makes the potential economic upside worth something.
3. Journalists, partners, and more
We’ve talked about investors and employees, but there’s actually a long tail of many other constituents that benefit from a futuristic outlook. If you’re talking to journalists and pundits, you have to compete with thousands of other companies that they’re going to meet this year, and you have to catch their attention. If you’re marketing an event at a conference adjacent to dozens of other events, you have to catch the eye of attendees. An optimistic, futuristic perspective gives you room to tell the story about the problem you’re trying to solve and why your startup will be incredibly important once you get there.
You might say that the world is full of cynics. Perhaps you are from a region or an industry where most people nitpick all the reasons why you’ll fail. Maybe they want you to focus on minimizing downside risks, or acknowledging your potential problems, and won’t treat you as credible unless you do. If that’s your industry network or your social network, I urge you to escape. Seek out those who share your optimism and your values and beliefs about the future. It’s one of the reasons why the Bay Area has been such a powerhouse over the last few decades. Yes, there’s knowledge and investors here, but more important is the culture.
The last point I make is about yourself. You should talk about what you’re working on in an optimistic way to help create meaning for yourself. For those of us who grew up in a generation that adored Steve Jobs, there has always been the goal to put a dent in the universe rather than to sell sugar water. Thinking about the future—and the upside of what you might be able to create—is a great way to give meaning to the nights and blood and tears that we put into our work. If you’re working on a new product only because it’s a good money-making opportunity, I guarantee that your sense of meaning will fade when times get tough. You’ll ask yourself, why am I doing this? If you don’t have a North Star to guide you on an inevitably rocky, entrepreneurial journey, you’ll inevitably get lost. That’s when the FAANG job will seem really appealing.
Obviously, don’t drink your own Kool-Aid
This is all about the pitch. Of course it’s important to simultaneously hold in your mind all the truths about what you’re working on. Maybe you don’t actually have product market fit yet, or a marketing strategy. Perhaps your unit economics don’t yet work, or your team has major gaps. If you’re working on a new startup, likely, everything feels constantly broken, and everyone’s maybe going to quit.
You have to go and tackle all of those challenges with a clear mind—while simultaneously keeping a futuristic spirit that motivates people to join you on your journey.
Like this post? Forward it to your team! And be sure to sure to subscribe to Andrew Chen’s Substack [ https://substack.com/redirect/b35d5807-efca-48b8-a136-8a18ec453f08?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more essays like this one.
For more weekly dives into the world of early stage startups, subscribe to our own newsletter below.
Get the a16z speedrun newsletter weekly 👇

subject: Maximize your account security
body_text:
Qonto ( http://qonto.com/en )

Stay alert to fraud

Stay alert to fraud

Hello Meghdad,

Safeguarding your account is our top priority.

>From Strong Customer Authentification (SCA) to 3D Secure (3DS2), we’ve put the best security systems in place to keep your Qonto account safe.

But fraudsters will keep trying their luck, so we need you to stay vigilant against their most common techniques:

*
Phishing: scammers try to steal your personal information ( https://qonto.com/en/security?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Prevent_fraud&utm_content=phising_page_en ) via fake emails or login pages that imitate Qonto’s.
*
Spoofing: fraudsters pose as a Qonto advisor and call you ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us/articles/23949190237713?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=Prevent_fraud&utm_content=spoofing_faq_en ) - asking you to make a
transaction on your account. They also have tools to display any phone number or name, so it can even look like we’re calling you.
*
Investment scams: fake companies contact you, promising high and rapid returns on investment.

Stay one step ahead of fraudsters

In addition to Qonto’s security measures, you can protect yourself against fraudsters by adopting the right reflexes:

*
Never reveal your login credentials, bank details, or card numbers on unknown websites or to third parties - even if they claim to be from Qonto on a secure vocal server. We will never ask you for your SMS verification codes or to make a transfer.
*
Double check the website URL when you connect to Qonto. Bookmark the Qonto login page as a favorite in your browser - the authentic page will always have this exact URL: https://app.qonto.com/signin/ ( https://app.qonto.com/detectapp.html?appUrl=deeplinks%3Faction%3Dsignin )
*
Never approve a transaction or login to your account at the request of a third party. Qonto will never ask you to do this.
*
Carefully read the notifications and verification SMS messages you receive when transactions are made on your account. Make sure it was you who initiated them and that you recognize the IBAN and recipient names.

Help center ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=signature&utm_content=FAQ_en )

Have a question?

If you notice any unusual activity on your account, contact us immediately at support@qonto.com or by chat when you’re signed in to the app.

Go to help center ( https://support-de.qonto.com/hc/en-us?utm_source=qonto_internal&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=signature&utm_content=FAQ_en )

Qonto ( https://qonto.com/en )

We collect and process your personal data so we can send you updates on products and industry news. Learn more ( https://qonto.com/en/legal-documents/privacy-notice ) about how we manage your data and your rights.

Qonto is the commercial name of Olinda SAS, a simplified joint stock company under French law, registered in the Paris Trade Register under n° 819 489 626, with registered office in Paris, 18 rue de Navarin, 75009 Paris, VAT-ID: FR10819489626. Qonto is a payment institution regulated by the ACPR in France under number CIB 16958.
