subject: Andrew Chen on the Lost Art of Designing Viral Loops
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://speedrun.substack.com/p/the-lost-art-of-designing-viral-loops

💡  This Week’s Big Ideas
🧊  SR005 founder Philipp Wehn of Nexxa had a child shortly before Demo Day, but still managed to pull it all together. This is his story. [ https://substack.com/redirect/9f33b8f7-a7fc-40cf-8a9b-3d7f7888308a?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]
🔨  Tom Hammer comes in swinging [ https://substack.com/redirect/1aab3c99-5b0d-4b68-8389-64a9911c5f9b?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] with a different perspective on “996”
🔮  Bella Nazzari knows some startups that are hiring [ https://substack.com/redirect/41fbd5bf-8cef-4c1c-8274-da714544d299?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]…
🤝  Jon Lai makes the case for being more friendly [ https://substack.com/redirect/71e10bae-18d4-439f-ac67-e81c7458c7f9?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] as a founder
💼  Join our talent network [ https://substack.com/redirect/d10247c9-ea94-4cdf-af11-9a19112013b0?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more opportunities. If we see a fit for you, we'll intro you to relevant founders in the portfolio.
Over the last two weeks, a16z speedrun General Partner Andrew Chen has written a series of “brain dumps” on X [ https://substack.com/redirect/0e58dfca-be8b-4654-a202-38a9e135179f?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] about what he describes as a now-lost art of designing viral user acquisition loops for software products. 
Andrew will soon be posting all three essays on his Substack [ https://substack.com/redirect/2151d025-6529-403a-b6d9-102ff427f9e0?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], and we’re publishing the first in the series below.
The golden age of Web 2.0 (~2005-2010) was a special time for viral products, which were systematically engineered to reach millions of people. Back then, people were building the first versions of things we now take for granted: Social networks, user-generated platforms, collaborative workplace products, messaging apps, etc.
During this time, the industry developed a comprehensive and systematic understanding of creating viral loops. There was measurement, A/B testing, and equations that needed to be satisfied. Products optimized and optimized, engineering virality. And some of the most successful of these products eventually grew to billions of users and became recognizable names.
Then all of a sudden, it ended.
And funny enough, the people who were successful at creating viral products all became billionaires, or if they didn’t create successful companies, many became FAANG executives or VC investors. Eventually, all the knowledge around building virality was basically lost. This happened as Web 2.0 ended and the mobile era began.
I thought it might be nice to write down a full brain dump of everything that I learned about viral loops during this period, because it does continue to be relevant, although the mechanics and tactics around it keep shifting and changing. Yet the overall theory is still the same, and I think it can be extended into future technology platforms and marketing channels. It also has a high degree of applicability to this era’s Product-Led Growth, all the sharing flows of genAI apps, and referral programs that still exist within every marketplace/ecommerce product.
With these notes, I plan to cover a bunch of different ideas and topics:
Simple viral loops that work, but then quickly disintegrate.
Viral factor and how to calculate it.
How to actually break down a viral loop step by step and optimize
What increases the viral factor, and what decreases it?
How to improve viral factor
Case studies like content sharing, invite flows, referral loops, collaboration loops, etc.
How retention drives viral growth.
How do you incorporate retention into your viral factor calculation
Why new users invite more people than existing ones.
How to think about word-of-mouth vs. engineered virality.
How to think about paid marketing, SEO, social media, and the effects of other channels on viral factor.
Why mobile eventually killed the classic viral loops.
What kinds of viral loops work in the contemporary era
...and much more
If you have thoughts and questions please ask. Hopefully I’ll eventually turn this into a big PDF or something that people can read if they’re interested. Or maybe a deck if we have some kind of long-form text to PowerPoint deck genAI tool one day that’s actually good :)
VIRAL GROWTH AS AN EQUATION
Usually, when most people talk about viral growth, they mean something silly (and non-durable), like tweeting a really cool video that then gets shared a bunch of times and drives traffic to your product. That’s not what I’m talking about. Instead, the viral loop I’m talking about is designed into a product, with invite features, tagging, referral links, and so on.
These types of viral loops have interesting properties:
it is measurable and can be tracked
it can be improved with product decisions
the math applies to any form of product-driven virality (invites, shared content, referral programs, etc)
The first thing is trying to measure viral factor. The viral factor is a simple ratio—the usual thought experiment is that if you bring 100 users into your product, and those users invite and eventually sign up 150 users, then those will sign up 225 users and so on. This is a viral invite flow, and your viral factor in this case would be 1.5 (rarely seen in the real world). On the other hand, if your 100 users end up signing up 50 of their friends, who then sign up 25, then your viral factor is 0.5. When it’s <1 then your viral loop eventually stops working.
Very precisely, the viral factor is a RATIO. The denominator is the # of users within a time-bound cohort (like everyone who signed up from X date to Y date), and the numerator is how many users they eventually signed up via the viral loops from that cohort.
Let’s use an example to make it concrete.
CASE STUDY: CONTENT SHARING
A very common form of viral loop has an app that lets a user create something really cool (maybe that’s with AI or photo filters or otherwise). After the user creates something, they want to share it, so naturally a link is provided. Some new users receive this link, view the content, and some smaller % of these folks sign up to make their own content. At its core, this is what the new generative video and photo apps do (like Sora). But this is also the same viral loop that made Instagram successful with photo filters or what’s made blogging popular as well. After all, when you create some type of content, you naturally want to share it with people, but those people naturally may want to participate as well.
So conceptually, you might understand the smile loop, but the question is, what can you do with it to optimize?
To make it systematic, you want to track it. When someone makes a generative video, you want to track things via the URL structure:
productdotcom/vid/[video ID]?sharer_id=[sharer]
(Of course, your userID is encoded as the sharer ID whenever you share.) Now when you share it out via email, or messaging, or whatever, if an invitee gets the link, watches the video, and then signs up, you should then store the sharer’s ID alongside the user’s signup row. So imagine you’d store the usual stuff:
id
email
password
name
sharer_id <-- this is who caused this invite to happen
Now when you look at your rows and rows of users in this table, sometimes the share ID will be blank if that user just showed up and signed up on their own. However, if they are part of a viral loop, then their share ID will point to some other user that already exists in the table.
To calculate viral factor, you then take some cohort of users, for example, the ones that joined 3 months ago. Let’s say that there are 100 of these. Then, what you do is you grab that list of IDs and you ask the database how many times do those IDs show up as share IDs in new sign-ups that happen afterwards? Let’s say that’s 50. Then your viral factor at that moment in time is 50/100=0.5.
What about all those users with blank sharer IDs? I sometimes think of the users who just show up as “Gen 1 users” or “onramp users” and you should just discard these as part of your viral factor calculation. Instead, what’s interesting is to compare the ratio of Gen 3 and Gen 2 users. Or really, Gen N+1 and N, as long as N isn’t 1. It turns out this ratio actually tends to stabilize pretty nicely over time.
THE BIG QUESTIONS
Do this with your product, then you’re bound to ask, “Wow, this is cool, but how do I make this number go up?” and in particular, “How do I make this number go over 1?”
This is the right question to ask. The minute you realize these dynamics, you’ll realize that you can make product changes to increase that viral factor so that your product becomes more viral. For example, maybe you should ask new users to invite when they first come in, or you should make it easier to invite people because there’s a link that you can copy and paste or some other mechanical change that increases viral factor. This is one of the core insights that you can measure viral factor and that you can actually make product changes to increase it.
Because you can track and calculate this ratio, this means you can stick it onto a dashboard as well. As I described, it’s easy to do track, as long as you encode the user id into the URL that’s shared. Of course, as soon as you’re able to calculate it, the next thing is then to be able optimize it because then you can run A/B tests to see if more people share, and do they do more of them? Do they share with more people? And for the people who receive a growth link, what percentage of them convert, and how you make that conversion higher. This is sort of exists in the cookbook of all the different ways that you might be able to optimize your viral factor.
The final aspect of the viral loop idea that’s powerful is that it’s actually very generalizable. Which is why this is all so powerful for the modern era of Product-Led Growth. As long as you’re talking about being able for a group of users to somehow generate another group of users, then of course that growth process might be because they’re inviting other people. It might be because they are sharing a content link that then causes someone else to sign up. Or it might be a referral program or many other tactics that cause one user to invite another. This means that at the core, there ends up becoming a lot of applicability across marketing channels and strategies.
ALTERNATIVE WAYS TO CALCULATE VIRAL FACTOR
When you ask the Internet how to calculate viral factor, it gives you something slightly different, which looks more like this:
VIRAL FACTOR = # INVITES x % CONVERSION RATE
This definition has been floating out there for a long time. However, it’s flawed because not all viral loops exist as a type of invite. Instead, we see sharing flows, collaboration flows, referrals, and many other variations. I also think that although it’s mechanically correct, because increasing the number of invites and increasing the conversion rate naturally does help your viral factor. It does not capture the fact that really what you want to know is this ratio between two cohorts of users.
VIRAL LOOPS AND SPAM LOOPS
There is also a dark side of simply trying to increase these variables as well:
The more simplistic version of this equation pushes you towards thinking about how to be as spamming as possible. How do we get users to invite as many of their friends as possible? This sort of incentivizes pushing users to invite pushing them to invite a lot of people, and then for the invites to be spamming in such a way that there is a high conversion rate.
Going back to the Web 2.0 era, this actually was the way that people tended to think about virality. If you’ve built the bare bones of a social network as folks at Bebo, Tagged, Hi5, and MySpace did, you were generally growing your network based on getting people to invite their friends via email. In the early days, you’d actually ask people to type in their friends’ email addresses, and usually they’d type in like 5–10 emails since it was tedious. It turns out that if you could get them to import their address book from Hotmail or Yahoo Mail, by asking them for an email/password combo, then crawling their inbox, you could push the number of invites to 200+. Of course whatever viral factor you had, a 20x increase would often put you over the top.
The downside is that you would generally drive down your conversion rate because you were emailing dead emails. Eventually, all of the email providers would start labeling you as spam. But for ~10 years, the glorious email invite loop worked, and many large products were built—including ultimately the big winners, Facebook and LinkedIn.
CHAIN LETTERS AND WHY SPAM LOOPS FAIL
You might know that over a hundred years ago, there was a concept of a chain letter where people would write actual snail mail letters to each other. Each chain letter would have a list of other addresses, and it would tell you that if you mailed a nickel over to them, and then also added yourself to the list, then eventually everyone would get rich because you would be receiving an exponentially higher amount of nickels from people over time. This was actually such a big deal that eventually the post office had to make it illegal since it swamped all of their delivery. But of course, mathematically, chain letters are going to fail.
The reason is simple, which is saturation. If you have a viral loop and you invite 200 people on each go, eventually after a bunch of generations, you’re going to completely saturate your target market. If you do the math, once you pass 5 rounds of 200+ invites, then you’ve already hit every human on the planet and more. So eventually you’re just hitting the same people over and over again. Naturally, the response rate is going to go down. Why? 1) You’ve already signed them up, in which case that invite can’t convert. 2) Or, alternatively, they got invited before, but they’re not interested and they don’t interact. Either way, the response rate tends to decrease over time.
If the response rate goes down and your product is not retentive, then mathematically all you’ve done is created a huge spike of new users which looks good for a while, but then that lack of retention means that your total number of active users ends up being a tiny fraction of new users.
This is why some highly viable products end up jumping the shark. In the past, I’ve listed out some of these metrics to indicate that a product will be sticky:
cohort retention curves that flatten (stickiness)
actives/reg > 25% (validates TAM)
power user curve showing a smile -- with a big concentration of engaged users (you grow out from this strong core)
viral factor >0.5 (enough to amplify other channels)
dau/mau > 50% (it’s part of a daily habit)
market-by-market (or logo-by-logo, if SaaS) comparison where denser/older networks have higher engagement over time (network effects)
D1/D7/D30 that exceeds 60/30/15 (daily frequency)
revenue or activity expansion on a per user basis over time -- indicates deeper engagement / habit formation
>60% organic acquisition with real scale (better to have zero CAC)
For subscription, >65% annual retention (paying users are sticking)
>4x annual growth rate across topline metrics
These aren’t meant to be exhaustive, but they are all strong indicators that if a product can generate a lot of viral users, there’s enough there that it’ll stick. If not, then eventually the spike will go away.
The fact that you need product-market fit ends up being one of the reasons why sometimes you hear about highly viral apps that then disappear from public consciousness. You can actually have a very high viral factor or high word of mouth and sometimes you can even engineer it. But it’s almost like having a big bang launch or having a Super Bowl commercial or something like that. You get a huge spike and it’s great for a while, but inevitably, unless your ratio of active users to sign-ups is very high, it means that you’ll eventually lose them.
This was ultimately the outcome of many of the apps that were created during Web 2.0 or during the Facebook platform era. You heard about a lot of virality and potentially millions of users or even hundreds of millions of users, and sometimes this happened quickly. But a lot of this were people engineering viral loops. And as those users left the app because of a lack of stickiness, these viral loops could be engineered to try and reacquire them over and over again. The vast majority of these viral apps did not actually become successful businesses, which is why—although I remain very interested in the ability for teams to create growth hacks, of course—I also care a lot about their ability to actually retain users.
Like this post? Forward it to your team! And be sure to sure to subscribe to Andrew Chen’s Substack [ https://substack.com/redirect/758a1110-c83e-408d-af7f-e2891c8b9fb8?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more essays like this one.

subject: B2B SaaS – Product ready - Zalando SE paid pilot secured – Seed Round kEUR 600; Calibrion.ai - AI Infrastructure / Data annotation
body_text:
Dear Team at Backed Venture Capital,

We are building Calibrion, an agent-native data annotation platform adressing one of the largest barriers to organisational GenAI adoption: enabling proprietary LLM training through high-quality, rapid annotation. 

Calibrion delivers: 
- Agentic data annotation that accelerates annotation up to 170x (from months to hours)
- Cost savings of around 90%
- Built-in proprietary quality control to ensure accuracy at scale 
- 35% faster human-in-the-loop through research driven optimized workflows

We recently signed a 6 months paid pilot with Zalando SE, secured a verbal commitment from Siemens Energy AG E-Commerce division, and are negotiating pilots with Cotopaxi (US sustainable outdoor brand) and LILT (AI-driven translation leader). 

We are seeking EUR 600,000 in seed funding to expand our team, onboard additional clients, and implement product-led-growth.

Our founder and CEO, Meghdad, Holds a PhD in Computer Science with a specialization in Natural Language Processing, and has over eight years of applied AI product experience. 

Given your investment focus, we think Calibrion could be a strong fit. You’ll find our one-pager, pitch-deck, and product demos attached. We’d be glad to arrange a 30-minute call to walk you through the product and discuss and our upcoming milestones.

Best regards,
Nils 


Nils Erbach

Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
Tel: +49 1 <tel:+49+(0)30+478+662+25>7662205065 ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>
Kolonnenstr. 8・10827 Berlin・Germany
www.calibrion.ai <https://www.calibrion.ai/>
￼
￼
￼

subject: B2B SaaS – Product ready - Zalando SE paid pilot secured – Seed Round kEUR 600; Calibrion.ai - AI Infrastructure / Data annotation
body_text:
Dear Julia,

We are building Calibrion, an agent-native data annotation platform adressing one of the largest barriers to organisational GenAI adoption: enabling proprietary LLM training through high-quality, rapid annotation. 

Calibrion delivers: 
- Agentic data annotation that accelerates annotation up to 170x (from months to hours)
- Cost savings of around 90%
- Built-in proprietary quality control to ensure accuracy at scale 
- 35% faster human-in-the-loop through research driven optimized workflows

We recently signed a 6 months paid pilot with Zalando SE, secured a verbal commitment from Siemens Energy AG E-Commerce division, and are negotiating pilots with Cotopaxi (US sustainable outdoor brand) and LILT (AI-driven translation leader). 

We are seeking EUR 600,000 in seed funding to expand our team, onboard additional clients, and implement product-led-growth.

Our founder and CEO, Meghdad, Holds a PhD in Computer Science with a specialization in Natural Language Processing, and has over eight years of applied AI product experience. 

Given your investment focus, we think Calibrion could be a strong fit. You’ll find our one-pager, pitch-deck, and product demos attached. We’d be glad to arrange a 30-minute call to walk you through the product and discuss and our upcoming milestones.

Best regards,
Nils 


Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
Tel: +49 1 <tel:+49+(0)30+478+662+25>7662205065 ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>
Kolonnenstr. 8・10827 Berlin・Germany
www.calibrion.ai <https://www.calibrion.ai/>

- Cost savings of around 90%
- Built-in proprietary quality control to ensure accuracy at scale 
- 35% faster human-in-the-loop through research driven optimized workflows

We recently signed a 6 months paid pilot with Zalando SE, secured a verbal commitment from Siemens Energy AG E-Commerce division, and are negotiating pilots with Cotopaxi (US sustainable outdoor brand) and LILT (AI-driven translation leader). 

We are seeking EUR 600,000 in seed funding to expand our team, onboard additional clients, and implement product-led-growth.

Our founder and CEO, Meghdad, Holds a PhD in Computer Science with a specialization in Natural Language Processing, and has over eight years of applied AI product experience. 

Given your investment focus, we think Calibrion could be a strong fit. You’ll find our one-pager, pitch-deck, and a few product demos attached. We’d be glas to arrange a 30-minute call to walk you through the product and discuss and our upcoming milestones.

Best regards,
Nils 


Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
Tel: +49 17662205065 ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:nils@calibrion.ai>
Kolonnenstr. 8・10827 Berlin・Germany
www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>
￼￼ ￼

subject: Slide 47
from_name: Robin Guo from The Bird's Nest
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://www.robin-guo.com/p/slide-47

When we are kids the hero’s journey is all around us. We read about Frodo’s journey through Middle Earth, we watch Luke realize his father is Darth Vader, we follow Luffy as he unlocks his hidden powers throughout the Grand Line. And everything is an adventure when you’re a kid. The playground is a colorful castle. A family trip is always to somewhere new and novel. Teenage angst is part of the beauty of growing up.
But then you grow up. You graduate. You find a real job that pays the bills. You go into the same office every day, eat the same food, drink the same coffee. You get into routine. You work your way up the corporate ladder. You have a mortgage, bills, spouse, and kids to worry about.
Where did the time go?
I remember the exact moment I knew I was slowly dying. Week ten at a pharmaceutical company in central New Jersey. Another Wednesday. I was eating Farmer’s Fridge again, a small Caesar wrap dispensed from a modern millennial vending machine, for the fourth time this week because that was the best option at the cafeteria. My laptop displayed slide 47 of a deck titled “Organizational Synergies Post-Merger.” I was adjusting the formatting on a text box about “leveraging cross-functional capabilities.”
This was it. This was what twenty-four years of preparation had led to. A very prestigious purgatory. I was making $100K a year to slowly transform into furniture.
I originally chose consulting because I thought it was the adventure. Fly around the world. Give advice to some of the top F500 executives. Work with some of the smartest people around.
All true.
But also what was true was endless slides, endless meetings, and endless optimization. I had this nagging feeling I wasn’t… building anything real.
Maybe this was just modern life? And I’d have to get used to it? Maybe the fantasies I had as a kid of galactic exploration, horseback rides through the vast plains, pitched battles with mysterious foes was just that… fantasy.
Call it idealism or call it naiveté but that’s why I moved to California. To chase the modern gold rush. To work in gaming, a literal fantasy. I wanted to answer the call to adventure.
What I wasn’t prepared for was the struggle, the pain that is required for any adventure. I was always prepared to work hard, but I wasn’t quite ready for the market turning for the worse, the uncertainty about what to do next, the lack of meaning, the depression, the feeling of inadequacy, the responsibility of carrying important decisions.
The hero’s journey always has that moment. When Frodo stands at Mount Doom. When Luke faces Vader. When you’re three years into the California dream, unsure about what’s next, wondering if you made a catastrophic mistake leaving everything you knew: friends, family, job, safety, and home.
And then recently one of my founders said to me: “Speedrun feels like summer camp. Three months of crazy adventures and now we’re all going home.”
And what I realized over the last year was that my journey here in California was about helping others realize their journey. To build a structure, program, capital, and community to enable others to make it through.
See, what actually kills adventures isn’t the dark lord. It’s the Tuesday at 2 AM when you’re alone, your runway is shrinking, and nobody understands why you left your tech company to do this crazy idea. It’s the isolation and self doubt.
But when you’re with five other people that are all having the same Tuesday at the same time? That’s different. Someone’s been there. Someone has an idea. Someone reminds you why you started.
The specifics matter less than the shared struggle: whether it’s debating human behavior until 2AM at the Airbnb, or that moment when a founder from Zurich realizes a founder from New York is dealing with the same customer challenges, or when someone gets their first 1M in revenue and the whole cohort celebrates like they just summited Everest.
We built speedrun to be base camp for the greatest adventure of our time: creating something from nothing. Every three months, a hundred people remember that the ten-year-old who thought they could change the world wasn’t wrong, just early.
So to all those still sitting in their corporate companies, adjusting text boxes on slide 47, eating the same chicken Caesar wrap:
The adventure isn’t gone. It’s waiting.
It will cost you everything comfortable.
You will lose sleep, money, and certainty.
You will become someone your current colleagues won’t recognize.
But the alternative is worse. It’s 3 PM on a Tuesday in ten years, and you’re on slide 48, and the wrap tastes exactly the same, and the ten-year-old version of you has stopped trying to get your attention because they know you’re not listening anymore.
My hero’s journey ended where it was supposed to begin: helping others start theirs.
The Bird's Nest is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

Calendly’s browser extension for Safari (new!), Chrome, Firefox,
and Edge lets you book and join meetings from anywhere on the
web. Share your links, offer available time slots, and manage

subject: Offer to join Antler
body_text:
Hi Meghdad,

*Congratulations on receiving an offer to join Antler.*

*You have been chosen from thousands of talented Founders,* from all over
the world, to be part of the coming residency!

We really enjoyed our recent discussions and we are thrilled to welcome you
to our residency.

*So, what is next?*

You will shortly receive a Letter of Offer via DocuSign. The letter lays
out details of the residency and details of funding.

Once you accept the Letter of Offer, you will receive an email preparing
you to start in the upcoming cohort with your fellow founders.

*Please note that you will have 7 days to sign the agreement, so do not
hesitate to let Matteo know if you have any questions! You can reach him at
matteo.condello@antler.co <matteo.condello@antler.co>*

We highly recommend you to sign the Letter of Offer as soon as possible, to
reserve your spot and be onboarded in the pre-residency as soon as it
starts.

*During the pre-residency, you will have the advantage* of already meeting
with all the other founders that are joining the next cohort, and starting
the search for your co-founder. Moreover, we will host exclusive ideation
sessions and coaching sessions for founders who have already signed the
agreement, so that they can get feedback on their ideas early on.

By the way, here are just a few examples that raised significant pre-seed
rounds from renowned VCs after Antler’s backing and with Antler’s support:

   - *NetBird*, a Berlin based open-source network security platform, has
   just recently raised €4 million in seed funding to scale its operations
   from top investors like InReach Ventures and Nauta Capital.
   - *Climate Tech NeoCarbon*, based in Berlin, have raised €5m seed
   funding since Antler’s initial investment in 2021.
   - *Fero*, an Antler company based in Amsterdam, recently raised a total
   of US$3M, led by a top US investor.
   - *Simplyblock*, based in Berlin, recently raised €2.5M within 11 months
   of completing the Antler residency. They enable cheaper and faster high
   performance cloud storage thus improving the carbon footprint of data
   centers.
   - *Genie*, an Antler company from Amsterdam, raised $1.2M in their seed
   round.

We look forward to building a high-impact groundbreaking company with you!

I am looking forward to meeting you here in the office and in the meantime,
please do not hesitate to let me know if you have any questions/doubts we
can help with!

Have a great day ahead and looking forward to hearing from you!

Kind regards,

Alan

--

to whom they are addressed (or meant to be addressed to). If you are not

to the intended recipient, be advised that you have received this email in

its attachments, is confidential and sent to provide information for

an invitation, offer or solicitation to purchase or subscribe for any 
investment.

subject: Re: Cotopaxi x Calibrion - AI photo generation initiative @ Tue, Nov 4, 2025 10:30am – 11:00am (GMT-08)
body_text:
Hi Eugene,

I hope you are doing well!

We’re expanding Calibrion’s e-commerce use cases, and your workflow is a
particularly strong fit.

Would you be open to a 3-month pilot with our platform for your product
image generation needs?
We will optimize the flows to match your needs and offer
additional services, flows and consultancy where needed to ensure your
requirements are met. Since the main focus of our pilots is currently
solving your e-commerce needs, not revenue, we can structure the pilot at a
very low cost, and we are completely flexible there.

If this is something you're interested in, let's jump on a call and discuss
further.

Best,
Meghdad



On Fri, Dec 12, 2025 at 2:33 PM Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai>
wrote:

> Hi Eugene,
>
> I hope you are doing well.
>
> I was wondering if you had a chance to internally review the project quote
> from December 4th.
> Please let me know if you have any further questions or if you are ready
> to proceed.
>

subject: Re: Cotopaxi x Calibrion - AI photo generation initiative @ Tue, Nov 4, 2025 10:30am – 11:00am (GMT-08)
body_text:
You’re welcome, Eugene!

You can run it locally as we don’t use large models. In that case no costs.
If you choose to run it on a cloud server e.g. AWS usually minimal (less
than $50/month) as the solution doesn’t need GPU. We also won’t use any
paid API except at the beginning for selecting regions. So you also won’t
have any external API costs.

Let me know if you have any other questions.

Best,
Meghdad

body_text:
hi Meghdad, happy to have a call to discuss, but I think we look at this
run as a POC so to speak, with the 35L pack being the first product for AI
photography.  If it works well and growth is there, we ask you and your
team to build out a new workflow for other products in the line.  Also,
each image should be static, at least that is the direction I have been
given so far and no API is needed, I think we could have these store via
SFTP or to a google drive location.

Please let me know what you think and if you need a call.

Regards,
Eugene

subject: Re: Cotopaxi x Calibrion - AI photo generation initiative @ Tue, Nov 4, 2025 10:30am – 11:00am (GMT-08)
body_text:
Hi Eugene,

Thank you for sharing the project specifications and for your patience.

We discussed the project internally and completed an initial scoping.

However, the team still has a few questions before we can send you an offer:

1. Should we build the system for a single fixed front image with
predefined regions and variable XLSX/CSV color inputs, or should the image
and regions themselves be configurable? If configurable, should each
collection support one image or multiple images?

2. If you choose the second option from my question above, we’ll need to
build a more complex UI, starting with a quick prototype that we’ll review
with you for feedback and adjustment. Otherwise, we can provide a
lightweight Python-based UI for essential interactions, such as uploading
colors and exporting generated images.

3. Should the system include an API, and where would you like it to be
deployed?

Let me know if you have any questions or would like to schedule a meeting.
Best,
Meghdad

colors on those elements will not change. Feel free to let me know if you
have any questions or concerns.

subject:
[Action Advised] Review Workspace apps in the Gemini app admin
 console setting before GA by the end of April 2025
body_text:
Google Workspace logo



Workspace apps in the Gemini app will be enabled by default at GA.



Dear administrator,

We're writing to let you know that the Google Workspace apps (formerly  
Google Workspace extensions) setting in the Gemini app will transition from  
beta to general availability (GA) by the end of April 2025.

This change will enable the Gemini app to interact with Calendar, Docs,  
Drive, Gmail, Keep, and Tasks, providing more context to your prompts.

We've provided additional information below about this change.

What this means for your organization

Key Change:
When the Workspace apps setting becomes generally available, it will be  
turned “ON” by default. This will override the current Workspace apps Beta  
setting.

Potential Impact:
Starting today, admins can now configure the new “Allow access to Workspace  
apps after Beta (upcoming general availability)” setting in preparation for  
the GA rollout. This setting is available under the existing Workspace apps  
Beta settings.

What you need to do

No action is required if you want Workspace apps to remain “ON” for users  
with the Gemini app enabled.

Advised Actions:


Review and update the Workspace apps setting before the GA to prepare for  
this change. If you don't take any action, all users in your organization  
will automatically have access to Workspace apps in the Gemini app after GA.
If you have customized settings for the Workspace apps in Gemini by user,  
group, or organizational unit (OU) and want to retain them after GA, apply  
your current settings to the new “Allow access to Workspace apps after Beta  
(upcoming general availability)” setting.


Note: This update will have no impact on your current Gemini app service  
setting.

We're here to help

For more information on the admin console setting that allows users to  
connect Workspace apps to the Gemini app, please refer to the documentation  
in our Help Center.

Thanks for choosing Google Workspace.

– The Google Workspace Team




Was this information helpful?

A smiling face A neutral face A sad face

You have received this important update about your Google Workspace account  
because you designated this email address as a primary or secondary contact  
for mandatory service communications in your Google Admin console profile.

subject:
Pricing, Business Plan and Funding Efforts / Capital Raise - SAAS
 Agreement / Offer Letter Updated / Invoice draft
body_text:
Key Takeaways:

 - Some previous work I did on pricing … 


1. Pricing Strategy / Research & Insights 

- Three crucial components of a profitable SaaS pricing strategy: 
i) Pricing model to balance value and revenue - ability to help customers and being failry compensated for the help; 
ii) Pricing strategies used to achieve growth goals 
iii) Psychological pricing tactics used to fine-tune the price 

A. Pricing Schemes Available / Commonly Used: 

i) Flat rate pricing 

ii) Usage based pricing / Pay as you go model - Pricing can go up and down [ Companies are charged based on the number of API requests; transactions processed; gigabites data used ]

iii) Tiered pricing strategy - Each tier is designed around the needs (Budget) of a different type of potential customer 


iv) Per User Pricing / Per seat pricing: 


v) Per Active User Pricing / only actual users will be billed for the service 

￼

vi) Per Feature Pricing / Using Features as a value metric instead of users 


vii) Freemium Business Model 

B. Pricing Strategies availabe:

i) Penetration Pricing / reducing prices to rapidly gain adoption in a target market / serucre first mover adavantage / goal: Upselling and crossselling in the medium- to long term / i.e. Slack / Relic 


ii) Captive pricing / charging lower than expected for a ‚core‘ product & charging extra for additional products required to get the most out of the original product / i.e. modern printers and cartridges / i.e. Adobe offering old products almost for free, but deleting backward-compatibility, forcing users to upgrade if they wish to collaborate  

iii) Skimming pricing - Initially charging a high price and lowering over time / called: riding down the demand curve; 


iv) Prestige pricing - Strategy of maintaining high prices / goal: convey a sense of quality, exclusivity of luxury / companies can use it to develop a (relatively) small base of high-value customers / usable if you are a well-known brand & product is used by high profile companies you can differentiate yourself by reputation, allowing to charge a higher price 


v) Free trial pricing - 


vi) Cost plus pricing 


vii) Value based pricing 


C. Psychological Pricing Tactics: 

i) Price anchoring 


ii) Charm pricing 


iii) Odd-Even Pricing 


iv) Product Bundle Pricing 


v) High-Low Pricing 


vi) Trial Pricing 


vii) Analysis Paralysis 


viii) Decoy Pricing 


ix) Center Stage Effect 



2. Business Plan Calibrion AI 

i) Unit Economics [ For each pricing scheme: Team / Business / Enterprise ] 


ii) Top-Line 


iii) Cash-Flow and profitability / Break-Even 



3. Cap Raise and Funding 


i) Valuation 


ii) Finding the right money / VCs / Angel 


iii) Approaching / Term Sheet / Closing 



4. Operational To Do’s 


i) Tax 


ii)

subject: Receipt for subscription payment May 28, 2025
Fwd: Seed Round Subscription Opportunity Calibrion AI
body_text:
> Begin forwarded message:
> 
> From: "Fritz Kathrin (RBVC/EC)" <Kathrin.Fritz@de.bosch.com>
> Subject: AW: Seed Round Subscription Opportunity Calibrion AI
> Date: 28. August 2025 at 16:24:23 CEST
> To: Nils Erbach <nils@calibrion.ai>
> 
> Dear Nils,
>  
> thank you very much for reaching out to us. After examination by a colleague from our investment team, unfortunately I have to inform you that the project does not fit with our current strategic focus.
>  
> We wish you and your team all the best!
>  
> Mit freundlichen Grüßen / Best regards
> 
> Kathrin Fritz 
> 
> Executive Assistant (RBVC/EC) 
> Robert Bosch Venture Capital GmbH | Postfach 10 60 50 | 70049 Stuttgart | GERMANY | www.rbvc.com <http://www.rbvc.com/>
> Tel. +49 711 811-54613 | Kathrin.Fritz@de.bosch.com <mailto:Kathrin.Fritz@de.bosch.com>
> 
> Sitz: Gerlingen, Registergericht: Amtsgericht Stuttgart, HRB 724692 
> Geschäftsführung: Philipp Rose, Dr. Ingo Ramesohl 
> 
> ​
> Von: Nils Erbach <nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:nils@calibrion.ai>>
> Gesendet: Mittwoch, 27. August 2025 17:05
> An: RBVC Info (RBVC/EC) <Info.RBVC@de.bosch.com <mailto:Info.RBVC@de.bosch.com>>
> Cc: Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>>
> Betreff: Re: Seed Round Subscription Opportunity Calibrion AI
>  
> Dear team at Bosch Ventures,
> 
> I hope you’re doing well. I wanted to kindly follow up on my earlier email about Calibrion AI and our ongoing seed investment round.
> 
> At Calibrion, we’re addressing one of the hardest bottlenecks in building and customizing large language models: data annotation and high-quality training datasets. Our platform is built natively for LLMs, not adapted from legacy ML tooling, which allows us to deliver:
> 
> Significant time and cost savings – up to 35% lower annotation costs and weeks faster turnaround for enterprises.
> Unmatched data quality – powered by proprietary quality-control workflows.
> Early traction – pilot projects secured with a publicly traded German online retailer and a $150M-revenue US outdoor apparel brand.
> Huge market opportunity – with a serviceable obtainable market of ~$1–1.4B by 2028, driven by enterprises moving toward domain-specific LLM customization.
> Given Bosch Ventures’ focus on transformative, enabling technologies, we believe there’s a strong strategic fit here.
> We’d be delighted to walk you through our live product demo and discuss how Calibrion is positioning itself to be the market leader in LLM-specific data annotation. Would you have availability next week for a short introductory call? I’d be more than happy to accommodate your schedule.
> 
> Thank you for considering, and I look forward to the chance to connect.
> 
> Best regards,
> Nils
> —
> 
> Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
> Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 <tel:+49+(0)30+478+662+25> ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:meghdad@calibrion.ai>
> Französische Str. 20・10117 Berlin・Germany
> www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>
>  
> 
> 
> On 1. Aug 2025, at 09:47, Nils Erbach <nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:nils@calibrion.ai>> wrote:
>  
> Dear team at Bosch Ventures,
> 
> I hope this message finds you well.
> 
> I wanted to follow up on my previous email regarding Calibrion AI and our ongoing seed investment round. We’re excited about the interest we’ve received so far and would love the opportunity to present our live product to you in more detail.
> 
> As mentioned, we’re building a leading platform for LLM customization and domain adaptation, with strong early traction and pilot-stage engagements in the EU and US. Given Bosch Ventures’ track record in backing transformative technologies, we believe this could be a great fit for your portfolio.
> 
> If you’re interested, I’d be glad to share our detailed pitch deck and arrange a call or demo session at your convenience.
> 
> Do you have availability next week for a brief conversation? I’m happy to accommodate your schedule.
> 
> Looking forward to your feedback and hoping we can connect soon.
> 
> Best regards,
> Nils
> 
> --
> Finance & Legal ・ Calibrion AI
> Tel: +49 (0)30 478 662 25 ・ nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:nils@calibrion.ai>
> Französische Str. 20・10117 Berlin・Germany
> www.calibrion.ai <http://www.calibrion.ai/>
> 
> 
> On 22. Jul 2025, at 19:21, Nils Erbach <nils@calibrion.ai <mailto:nils@calibrion.ai>> wrote:
>  
> Dear team at Bosch Ventures,
>  
> I am reaching out to introduce Calibrion AI, a data annotation platform enabling enterprises and organisations to fine-tune LLMs to their proprietary data. We built a working product and already reached pilot-stage interest from European and US companies in the eCom vertical.
>  
> While most LLM infra and dev platforms focus on model architecture and LLMOps, we focus on unlocking enterprise adoption through (proprietary) data and fine-tuning workflows.
>  
> Calibrion envisions a future where businesses harness their proprietary data and domain expertise to build specialized LLMs, turning generative AI from a threat into a strategic asset for both internal and market-facing applications.
>  
> Our mission is to become a market-leading platform for LLM customization and domain adaptation, setting the standard for performance and usability in the enterprise AI space.
>  
> To underscore the scale of this opportunity and the importance of what Calibrion does: In June 2025, Meta acquired a 49% stake in Scale AI (a company also focused on LLM data annotation and founded in 2016) for $14.8 billion at a $30 billion valuation. 
>  
> We are currently offering subscription for our EUR 3 m seed investment round to: 
> - expand our engineering and data science team
> - Accelerate go-to-market efforts across verticals 
> - Further develop our AI capabilities and integrate new LLM-driven features.
>  
> To accelerate the company's development, we assigned significant equity to an option pool for key employees and aim to be cash-flow positive within 18 months.

>  
> We look forward to hearing from you and would be glad to schedule a conversation at your convenience.
>  
> Best regards,
> Nils

subject: Re: Data Annotation / Calibrion
body_text:
Hi Tofigh,

Since it's been a while, I am just checking in to see if there’s any update
about Calibrion procurement.
Let me know if there’s anything I can do to help move things forward.

Thanks so much,
Meghdad


On Tue, Mar 4, 2025 at 12:47 PM Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai>
wrote:

> No worries, thanks for the update!
>
> On Tue, Mar 4, 2025 at 12:16 PM Tofigh Naghibi <tofigh.naghibi@zalando.de>
> wrote:
>
>> Hi Meghdad,
>> I am afraid we are still stuck at the very first approval. The SVP who is
>> required to approve the budget has still not given us a green light to
>> move forward.
>> Cheers,
>> T
>>
>> On Tue, 4 Mar 2025 at 11:40, Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai>
>> wrote:
>>
>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>
>>> I hope you're doing well.
>>>
>>> I'm just checking in to see if there have been any updates regarding the
>>> procurement process for Calibrion.
>>> If things are moving forward and they seem on-track to start this
>>> project, is it possible to provide me with an estimated starting date?
>>>
>>> Thank you very much in advance.
>>>
>>> Best regards,
>>> Meghdad
>>>
>>>
>>>
>>> On Wed, Feb 19, 2025 at 12:58 PM Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai>
>>> wrote:
>>>
>>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>>
>>>> I hope you're doing well.
>>>>
>>>> I'm just checking in to see if there have been any updates on your end
>>>> regarding Calibrion.
>>>> Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have any questions or need
>>>> anything from me.
>>>>
>>>> Thanks in advance and best regards,
>>>> Meghdad
>>>>
>>>>
>>>>

>>>>> No worries, Tofigh, and thank you very much!

>>>>> Meghdad

>>>>>
>>>>>> Hi Meghdad,
>>>>>> Owe you an apology on my end, I got busy with many things and didn't
>>>>>> follow up. Let me try to accelerate it.
>>>>>> Best,
>>>>>> T
>>>>>>
>>>>>> On Wed, 29 Jan 2025 at 11:18, Meghdad Farahmand <meghdad@calibrion.ai>
>>>>>> wrote:
>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> I hope you had a great start into 2025!
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> I'm just following up on our previous conversation regarding
>>>>>>> Calibrion. Please let me know if there are any updates from procurement or
>>>>>>> if you have any questions.
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> Best regards,
>>>>>>> Meghdad
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>> On Fri, Dec 13, 2024 at 8:27 AM Meghdad Farahmand <
>>>>>>> meghdad@calibrion.ai> wrote:
>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> Sounds great, thank you for the update!
>>>>>>>> Have a great weekend!
>>>>>>>> Meghdad
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>> On Fri, Dec 13, 2024 at 8:20 AM Tofigh Naghibi <
>>>>>>>> tofigh.naghibi@zalando.de> wrote:
>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> Hi Meghdad,
>>>>>>>>> I have requested procurement for now no action is needed. let’s
>>>>>>>>> wait alittle bit. The discussion regarding the terms of the contract comes
>>>>>>>>> later
>>>>>>>>> T
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>> On Friday, 13 December 2024, Meghdad Farahmand <
>>>>>>>>> meghdad@calibrion.ai> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> I hope you've had a wonderful week so far!
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> I am following up to see if you’ve made a decision regarding the
>>>>>>>>>> next steps.
>>>>>>>>>> Just to reiterate, we're quite flexible. We can accommodate
>>>>>>>>>> whichever approach suits you best, whether that's a usage-based monthly
>>>>>>>>>> plan or a pilot program.
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Looking forward to hearing back from you!
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> Best regards,
>>>>>>>>>> Meghdad
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>> On Thu, Dec 5, 2024 at 5:54 PM Meghdad Farahmand <
>>>>>>>>>> meghdad@calibrion.ai> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> Thank you again for the great meeting!
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> I can certainly see the benefits of your proposal.
>>>>>>>>>>> No problem to bill you only those months that you use Calibrion,
>>>>>>>>>>> but I think it is best to have at least 6 months of usage.
>>>>>>>>>>> Following what we discussed, this is an ideal minimum period for
>>>>>>>>>>> the return on your investment. Shorter than that might be a bit difficult
>>>>>>>>>>> to do.
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> What do you think about agreeing on (ideally) 6 months (or if
>>>>>>>>>>> that's not feasible 5 months) of usage, and then, those usage months can
>>>>>>>>>>> span over one year?
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> I am looking forward to hearing back from you!
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> Best regards,
>>>>>>>>>>> Meghdad
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>> On Thu, Dec 5, 2024 at 5:04 PM Tofigh Naghibi <
>>>>>>>>>>> tofigh.naghibi@zalando.de> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>> Hi Meghdad,
>>>>>>>>>>>> Just one thing came to my mind after our discussion, one thing
>>>>>>>>>>>> that certainly simplifies our processes and also the contract quite a bit
>>>>>>>>>>>> is that you bill us monthly rather than a pilot type contract.
>>>>>>>>>>>> Those months that we have a usage for the tool, you will bill
>>>>>>>>>>>> us 4k and for those that we don't it will have no cost for us. This has a
>>>>>>>>>>>> lot more benefits.
>>>>>>>>>>>> A) We can much quicker start the collaboration as these kind of
>>>>>>>>>>>> services are much quicker to be approved than those that require
>>>>>>>>>>>> integration with Zalando systems / Pilots
>>>>>>>>>>>> B) We bypass the complexity of the fact that there are months
>>>>>>>>>>>> that we don't have a usecase
>>>>>>>>>>>> C) In parallel, you can work with other teams and bill them
>>>>>>>>>>>> according to their usage. You won't be tied to the price you negotiated
>>>>>>>>>>>> with zResearch.
>>>>>>>>>>>> Cheers,
>>>>>>>>>>>> T
>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>> On Wed, 4 Dec 2024 at 17:58, Meghdad Farahmand <
>>>>>>>>>>>> meghdad@calibrion.ai> wrote:
>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>> Hi Tofigh,
>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>> Likewise!
>>>>>>>>>>>>> Sounds great!
>>>>>>>>>>>>> I will send a calendar invite shortly.
>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>> See you tomorrow.
>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>> Best regards,
>>>>>>>>>>>>> Meghdad
>>>>>>>>>>>>>
>>>>>>>>>>>>>

Hi Tofigh,

I hope you're doing well.

I'm just checking in to see if there have been any updates regarding the
procurement process for Calibrion.
If things are moving forward and they seem on-track to start this project,
is it possible to provide me with an estimated starting date?

Thank you very much in advance.

Best regards,
Meghdad

any questions.

subject: Re: Data Annotation / Calibrion
body_text:
Hi Tofigh,

Thank you again for the great meeting!

I can certainly see the benefits of your proposal.
No problem to bill you only those months that you use Calibrion, but I
think it is best to have at least 6 months of usage.
Following what we discussed, this is an ideal minimum period for the return
on your investment. Shorter than that might be a bit difficult to do.

What do you think about agreeing on (ideally) 6 months (or if that's not
feasible 5 months) of usage, and then, those usage months can span over
one year?

I am looking forward to hearing back from you!

Best regards,
Meghdad

subject: Re: Data Annotation / Calibrion
body_text:
Hi Meghdad,
Just one thing came to my mind after our discussion, one thing that
certainly simplifies our processes and also the contract quite a bit is
that you bill us monthly rather than a pilot type contract.
Those months that we have a usage for the tool, you will bill us 4k and for
those that we don't it will have no cost for us. This has a lot more
benefits.
A) We can much quicker start the collaboration as these kind of services
are much quicker to be approved than those that require integration with
Zalando systems / Pilots
B) We bypass the complexity of the fact that there are months that we don't
have a usecase
C) In parallel, you can work with other teams and bill them according to
their usage. You won't be tied to the price you negotiated with zResearch.
Cheers,
T

Dear Tofigh,

It was great to meet you the other day!

I am writing to follow up on our chat about your data annotation needs and
our platform Calibrion.

Do any of the following times work for you to have a 1-hour meeting to
present Calibrion and discuss your annotation needs?

Thursday: Anytime between 12pm and 4pm.
Friday: 10am or anytime after 3pm.

If none of these times work for you, please let me know so that I propose
other time slots.

Looking forward to speaking with you.

Best regards,
Meghdad

subject: Receipt for subscription payment Aug 28, 2025
Your Amazon Web Services Free Tier expires soon
body_text:
Hello,


Read carefully and take action to prevent unwanted charges.


The 12-month Amazon Web Services Free Tier period associated with your Amazon Web Services account 339712809871 will expire on February 28, 2025. If no action is taken, your resources will continue to run, and you’ll be automatically billed for any active resources when the 12-month Free Tier period ends.


We strongly advise that you sign in and review your Amazon Web Services Billing & Cost Management Dashboard to locate any active resources on your account that you no longer need. Even if you aren’t using your Amazon Web Services account or have closed the account, it’s possible that you still have active resources.


1. Go to your Billing Dashboard to see the line items by region for each service contributing to your Free Tier usage for the month.
Tip: Select each service or the ‘Expand All’ option to view all active services by region.

2. If you no longer need the resources, terminate them to prevent unwanted charges.

3. Open the Management Console, select the region in the navigation bar where you have any unwanted resources. Enter each service name in the search bar to open its dashboard. Terminate any unwanted resources. Please refer to this guide for detailed steps.
Note: Remember to terminate unwanted resources for each region. Terminating resources in one region will not lead to termination of those resources in other regions.

4. Monitor your Free Tier expiration. Once your short-term trials or 12-month Free Tier period ends, you’ll be charged standard, pay-as-you-go service rates for any active resources.

Sincerely,

Amazon Web Services

subject: The surprising new growth channel used by the smartest founders
body_text:
View this post on the web at https://speedrun.substack.com/p/3-tips-before-you-go-to-tech-week

If you’re in San Francisco this week, please join us at the a16z headquarters for a chance to meet the hottest AI B2B startups coming out of the last five batches of a16z speedrun! You’ll have the opportunity to meet dozens of SR founders and learn about (and demo!) the AI tools they’re building. The AI Faire is intended for executives looking to adopt new tech at their company. Sign up on Partiful [ https://substack.com/redirect/8635d410-5416-42be-848a-d396da5153c2?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. 
💡  This Week’s Big Ideas
🧊  Come meet our team at Tech Week SF (Oct 6–10) and LA (Oct 13–17)! See the full list of a16z speedrun events for San Francisco [ https://substack.com/redirect/3f4e3030-a008-497f-8ade-55c7daa114b8?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] and Los Angeles [ https://substack.com/redirect/09e4374f-524d-4e03-89ad-1f544568c575?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
💫  More amazing startups from the class of SR005 launched this week. Check out these viral launch videos from Lekondo [ https://substack.com/redirect/5635a202-a320-40de-95ad-1436f0fc1e58?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], Vega [ https://substack.com/redirect/8fd113d4-8ac2-4e05-906f-01b8b9356dbd?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], Fearn [ https://substack.com/redirect/b52361f4-cb7e-4102-bc97-fa109769178b?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], Sonatic [ https://substack.com/redirect/5089b39f-75bd-4964-9993-493a6367aa2b?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], and Kintow [ https://substack.com/redirect/e7ccbe6a-5510-4a39-b168-fff006ab0d72?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
🔨  Next Friday Tom Hammer is hosting a Startup Culture Brunch in Los Angeles! Come join other execs and founders as we discuss building strong, collaborative, and inclusive company cultures. Apply to join on Partiful [ https://substack.com/redirect/f529d08f-1f3f-48f1-8e06-0d846e9764be?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]. 
🤖  There are some great upcoming Tech Week panels about AI and creativity that include speedrun portcos. Check out the AI x Creativity panel feat. Hedra  [ https://substack.com/redirect/f05ea71f-7788-49a2-9d62-b4e25c0a1e6d?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]this Thursday. Then Promise is hosting Expanding the Boundaries of Storytelling with GenAI [ https://substack.com/redirect/d9d27416-51c3-4206-8e23-5d857fb9f643?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] next Thursday. 
💼  Join our talent network [ https://substack.com/redirect/273d0da9-626c-4f89-8860-6e4faff12efa?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] for more opportunities. If we see a fit for you, we'll intro you to relevant founders in the portfolio.
It’s Tech Week time.
What started only a few years ago as a loose get-together involving a confederation of LA hacker houses has evolved into one of the biggest open-source conference in all of tech, with 2,000 Tech Week events happening across SF and LA in the coming weeks. It is, as Tech Week creator Katia Ameri jokes, a hacker house that got out of hand and spread across America.
So what is Tech Week really all about, and why are founders, investors, and operators flocking to California this week? 
This week we spoke with the team behind Tech Week about the event’s origins as well as a few tips for attendees and hosts. Let’s get into it.
Tip #1: Use the TW Calendar to find Your Tribe
When Shrikala Kashyap [ https://substack.com/redirect/b2f4f87f-094e-4820-8990-13cee5dee588?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] first moved to San Francisco from India, she realized she needed a strategy to meet new people. “The city has notoriously tight social circles,” she says. “People either went to the same school, or grew up in the same neighborhood. So breaking into that required putting in some effort.”
Her first approach to try to find connections was by going to big conferences. But that didn’t work out well, in part because traditional conferences are so often purely about selling, or one-way communication between presenters and audiences. “It’s so formal that it’s really hard to actually meet people in that setting,” Kashyap says.
What worked instead were smaller, more curated gatherings—exactly the sorts of events put together by hosts at Tech Week.
“Tech Week is one of those places which is incredibly welcoming and open to newcomers,” Kashyap says. “You could be new to the city. You could be new to the industry, to the country, whatever. It is actually the fastest way to find your tribe.”
Because there are so many events at Tech Week, hosts have the freedom to get much more niche and specific with their target audiences. And when an event is a success, it often tends to turn into a recurring series at future Tech Weeks, allowing repeat attendees to form professional and personal relationships with real staying power.
So that’s tip #1: Finding your tribe is as easy as checking out the full list of events on the Tech Week calendar [ https://substack.com/redirect/1a86094f-272f-46ac-8608-66bd079b7154?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ].
Tip #2: Build Bridges to Other Cities’ Ecosystems
[calendar launch video embed]
When Katia Ameri [ https://substack.com/redirect/ce32c9f5-14e2-41e4-8cb2-27d7aa37dbe5?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] joined a16z, she hadn’t been looking for a job. After years of experience as both a founder (Mirra Skincare) and investor (Pear VC), she’d become  focused on community building in the LA tech ecosystem, first by starting a hacker house and then by organizing larger and larger get-togethers connecting to other hackers houses around LA.
Around 2021 she was contacted by a16z’s Andrew Chen [ https://substack.com/redirect/6f894323-7026-4da4-b473-c55b2525abaa?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ], who had gotten word that things were brewing in the LA tech ecosystem. “He kind of caught on to the fact that a lot of people were moving here and he was really interested in that. And so he was on the ground meeting people,” Ameri says.
Chen pitched Ameri on a new role, focused on developing a16z’s connections to the LA tech ecosystem, effectively building a bridge between SF tech and LA tech.
Quickly, the idea for a LA-focused tech conference came together. The big unlock was the idea of letting anyone submit their own events for inclusion on the official Tech Week calendar. “That’s when it went viral,” Ameri says. “We went from 30 to 300 events just in a couple weeks.”
By the end of that very first Tech Week, Ameri says, “We realized that there was product-market fit out here in LA for something like this. But it was also a way for us to build a bridge to connect our San Francisco ecosystem to Los Angeles in a lasting way.”
This is why now an explicit goal for the Tech Week team is what Ameri calls cross-pollination. “Each Tech Week is about celebrating that particular city with a big rah-rah moment,” she says. “But we hope that people will go outside their own cities as well. If you’re based in SF, we really want you to come to LA and experience the industries out here and vice versa. Same for New York. It’s important for us to have these moments that bring people together to actually meet and be able to exchange ideas and capital. We see that as how we can really help make a big dent in the growth of the ecosystem overall.”
There’s tip #2 - Get out of your own city and get connected to a whole broader world of people, ideas, and deals in places like New York and—Ameri hints—at least one additional new city that’ll play host to Tech Week next year.
Tip #3 - Level Up Your Marketing Strategy With Owned Events
Before joining the Tech Week team, Rose Johnson [ https://substack.com/redirect/991908f6-61b3-439b-9188-d5b962496f8e?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ] was leading marketing for a B2B SaaS fintech company (“super sexy,” she deadpans), and part of their GTM strategy involved going to big conferences. This, she says, was generally a frustrating experience.
“These conferences had set these prices, $100k for a 10 by 15 booth, and you could only use specific design specs,” she says. “If you wanted to do any kind of experiential activation, you had to ask them first. You could maybe get a popcorn machine in a specific slot. So we felt quite creatively handcuffed by the confinements of these big traditional conferences.”
When Johnson was invited to host an event at Tech Week, she was struck by how much more freedom the format allowed for. “You have the power to put on these wonderful experiences that you own end-to-end,” she says. “And these events tend to be far more enjoyable and higher ROI as well because people like going to the events more.”
Now, Johnson says, the Tech Week team is seeing far more teams, including startups, who are skipping big corporate conferences and beginning to allocate a higher portion of their marketing budgets to doing their own events. In some ways, she says, it mirrors what’s happening in media, with startups eschewing legacy press to build their own owned channels to reach audiences directly.
“There are so many parallels to what’s happening with new media,” Johnson says. “You used to only be able to get press in one way, and it was a very defined process of embargo, pitch, etc. But you were at the mercy of their discretion. Now people have the power to do their own thing. And now a similar thing is happening with events. There are all these intangible benefits all around it: the social noise you can make, it’s a branding exercise, and it’s a top of funnel motion as well.”
That’s tip #3: Get smart about owned events—before your competitors do it first.
That’s it for today. We hope to see you all at Tech Week. 
And remember, it’s never too late to become a Tech Week host! Check out the Tech Week website and submit your event today. [ https://substack.com/redirect/2e0fd8ab-8332-45db-a875-fd88de15aebc?j=eyJ1IjoiNTRjbDB4In0.woIuZ60MffrlGDsdpn_3Gb8spbUfXffYo0aHcFuwcjU ]
For more weekly dives into the world of early stage startups, subscribe to our own newsletter below.

subject: Meghdad, kurze Frage?
body_text:
Hi Meghdad,

deine Expertise im Bereich NLP und LLM hat mich beeindruckt – gerade weil ich mich aktuell intensiv damit beschäftige, wie Unternehmen ihre eigenen Daten optimal für KI nutzen können. Da musste ich einfach direkt Kontakt aufnehmen! 

Ich schätze, auch bei euch sorgt der Support für gut gefüllte To-Do-Listen.
Damit ich deine Zeit nicht verschwende, direkt zum Punkt.
Wir reduzieren euren Supportaufwand mit KI um bis zu 90% durch automatisierte Bearbeitung von Anfragen und Prozessen, die qualitativ auf Augenhöhe mit menschlichem Support sind oder diesen übertreffen.

Risiko? Du gehst keins ein. Wir entwickeln unverbindlich einen Prototypen auf unsere Kosten, damit ihr sofort seht, wie es für euch funktioniert.

Wenn das interessant klingt, lass uns einen kurzen Zoom Call machen.
Wenn nächste Woche grundsätzlich passt, richte ich mich nach dir.

Beste Grüße und einen schönen Tag
Jan 


Mitgründer | Phantom Automation GbR
Kirchstraße 17A, 79288 Gottenheim
+49 1573 5296426
jan@phantom-automation.de

Phantom Automation GbR
Gesellschafter: Dustin Geissinger Gromicho, Jan Paul Ruttkowski & Mike Andre Ruf 
Kirchstraße 17A, 79288 Gottenheim
+49 1516 8472604
info@phantom-automation.de
https://phantom-automation.de
 


Du kannst der weiteren Kontaktaufnahme jederzeit widersprechen. Eine kurze Antwort mit ‚Nein‘ genügt, und ich werde deine Kontaktdaten umgehend löschen. Danach wirst du keine weiteren E-Mails von mir erhalten.

Subject: Re: Connection - Private Placement Calibrion

Dear Louis-Armand,

Thank you for your email!

The NDA after the Easter holidays is perfectly fine with me.

Thank you for sharing this information about your recent placement. That sounds like a very thoughtful decision and quite helpful towards your client.

From my end, I thought given the duration of the process, it does not make any sense if we start the process, and after 3/4 months I have to drop off to find short-term financing/projects etc. to secure my runway.

I have started reaching out to a few BAs. Also an EX-CEO friend who has a good BA network in Berlin started to help me out. I think it looks good. I am looking for 250K, and with that, I can secure the runway for a year, add several interesting additions to the product, and build quite a bit of traction.
This I believe not only gives us time to find the right VC, but also if my traction plans work out, we can go for a higher round and valuation. I'll keep you posted.

Best regards,
Meghdad



On Mon, Apr 7, 2025 at 5:46 PM Louis-Armand Weisheimer - WEISSKNIGHT Corporate Finance <louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com<mailto:louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com>> wrote:
Good afternoon Meghdad,

I am really pleased to read you.

Also i realise i completely forgot to send you our corporate presentation. We need to put a simple nda in place (as all our past clients are in) but that is easy. I will do that as soon as i am back from the children easter holidays in probably 10 days (i hope this is ol for you).

The deal i mentioned to you during our last call. You have a great memory… well i actually advised my clients 2/3 weeks ago to put things on hold as the private equity buyer was too aggressive in the nego of the legal parameters and i was fearing the trump tarriff things/war… and i was not too wrong and my client is happy with the way i am managing the process (well i have to admit that i was not happy with the negotiation not in favour of my client, hen e why i wanted to slowdown - and given the mess since last week, we are very lucky to have slowdown things 🤣). Anyway all good and i am sure it will close later this year either with this fund or maybe with a strategic buyer (as in reality i never put things on hold and i am always hunting for new offers and alternatives).

I agree with you given your situation that it is better to delay further your process. Have you had any chance with a few business angels in Berlin? Or a german crowdfunding platform? Now that people are withdrawing money from the stockmarket, maybe more BA could be interested to invest 200/300k in your firm. That is what i wish you anyway for shortterm…

Bear in mind that a process takes in average 4/6 months when we push and the environment is normal. If we have more time to search for alternatives, then it is better. But linked with your email, our reduced engagement retainer of £10k still needs to be paid so you need to have financial bandwidth for sure. No rush. I like what you do and your philosophy as a businessman and hopefully we will work together at some points.

I will get back to you soon on the nda and our corporate presentation.

Thanks and kind regards
Louisarmand

Dear Louis-Armand,

I hope you're doing well. I was also wondering how the round you were about to close went.

I wanted to let you know that I would be thrilled to come back to you in the near future to start the private placement process. However, given that our runway is shortening, now is unfortunately not the right time for us. I'm hoping to be in a better position to revisit this in a few months.

I would also appreciate it if you could share your corporate pitch deck with me; I don't believe I have received it yet.

Best regards,
Meghdad

To: Louis-Armand Weisheimer - WEISSKNIGHT Corporate Finance <louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com>
Subject: Re: Connection - Private Placement Calibrion

Dear Louis-Armand,

Thank you for your email!

The NDA after the Easter holidays is perfectly fine with me.

Thank you for sharing this information about your recent placement. That sounds like a very thoughtful decision and quite helpful towards your client.

From my end, I thought given the duration of the process, it does not make any sense if we start the process, and after 3/4 months I have to drop off to find short-term financing/projects etc. to secure my runway.

I have started reaching out to a few BAs. Also an EX-CEO friend who has a good BA network in Berlin started to help me out. I think it looks good. I am looking for 250K, and with that, I can secure the runway for a year, add several interesting additions to the product, and build quite a bit of traction.
This I believe not only gives us time to find the right VC, but also if my traction plans work out, we can go for a higher round and valuation. I'll keep you posted.

Best regards,
Meghdad



On Mon, Apr 7, 2025 at 5:46 PM Louis-Armand Weisheimer - WEISSKNIGHT Corporate Finance <louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com<mailto:louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com>> wrote:
Good afternoon Meghdad,

I am really pleased to read you.

Also i realise i completely forgot to send you our corporate presentation. We need to put a simple nda in place (as all our past clients are in) but that is easy. I will do that as soon as i am back from the children easter holidays in probably 10 days (i hope this is ol for you).

The deal i mentioned to you during our last call. You have a great memory… well i actually advised my clients 2/3 weeks ago to put things on hold as the private equity buyer was too aggressive in the nego of the legal parameters and i was fearing the trump tarriff things/war… and i was not too wrong and my client is happy with the way i am managing the process (well i have to admit that i was not happy with the negotiation not in favour of my client, hen e why i wanted to slowdown - and given the mess since last week, we are very lucky to have slowdown things 🤣). Anyway all good and i am sure it will close later this year either with this fund or maybe with a strategic buyer (as in reality i never put things on hold and i am always hunting for new offers and alternatives).

I agree with you given your situation that it is better to delay further your process. Have you had any chance with a few business angels in Berlin? Or a german crowdfunding platform? Now that people are withdrawing money from the stockmarket, maybe more BA could be interested to invest 200/300k in your firm. That is what i wish you anyway for shortterm…

Bear in mind that a process takes in average 4/6 months when we push and the environment is normal. If we have more time to search for alternatives, then it is better. But linked with your email, our reduced engagement retainer of £10k still needs to be paid so you need to have financial bandwidth for sure. No rush. I like what you do and your philosophy as a businessman and hopefully we will work together at some points.

I will get back to you soon on the nda and our corporate presentation.

Thanks and kind regards
Louisarmand

body_text:
Dear Louis-Armand,

Thank you for your email!

The NDA after the Easter holidays is perfectly fine with me.

Thank you for sharing this information about your recent placement. That
sounds like a very thoughtful decision and quite helpful towards your
client.

>From my end, I thought given the duration of the process, it does not make
any sense if we start the process, and after 3/4 months I have to drop off
to find short-term financing/projects etc. to secure my runway.

I have started reaching out to a few BAs. Also an EX-CEO friend who has a
good BA network in Berlin started to help me out. I think it looks good. I
am looking for 250K, and with that, I can secure the runway for a year, add
several interesting additions to the product, and build quite a bit of
traction.
This I believe not only gives us time to find the right VC, but also if my
traction plans work out, we can go for a higher round and valuation. I'll
keep you posted.

Best regards,
Meghdad

Corporate Finance <louis-armand@weissknightcorporate.com> wrote:

> that is easy. I will do that as soon as i am back from the children easter
> holidays in probably 10 days (i hope this is ol for you).
>
> The deal i mentioned to you during our last call. You have a great memory…
> well i actually advised my clients 2/3 weeks ago to put things on hold as
> the private equity buyer was too aggressive in the nego of the legal
> parameters and i was fearing the trump tarriff things/war… and i was not
> too wrong and my client is happy with the way i am managing the process
> (well i have to admit that i was not happy with the negotiation not in
> favour of my client, hen e why i wanted to slowdown - and given the mess
> since last week, we are very lucky to have slowdown things 🤣). Anyway all
> good and i am sure it will close later this year either with this fund or
> maybe with a strategic buyer (as in reality i never put things on hold and
> i am always hunting for new offers and alternatives).
>
> I agree with you given your situation that it is better to delay further
> your process. Have you had any chance with a few business angels in Berlin?
> Or a german crowdfunding platform? Now that people are withdrawing money
> from the stockmarket, maybe more BA could be interested to invest 200/300k
> in your firm. That is what i wish you anyway for shortterm…
>
> Bear in mind that a process takes in average 4/6 months when we push and
> the environment is normal. If we have more time to search for alternatives,
> then it is better. But linked with your email, our reduced engagement
> retainer of £10k still needs to be paid so you need to have financial
> bandwidth for sure. No rush. I like what you do and your philosophy as a
> businessman and hopefully we will work together at some points.
>
> I will get back to you soon on the nda and our corporate presentation.
>
> Thanks and kind regards
> Louisarmand
